revision.text:
'''Kitabatake Shrine''' (北畠神社, ''Kitabatake jinja'') is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in [[Tsu, Mie Prefecture]], Japan. Its main festival is held annually on October 13. It was founded in 1643, and enshrines the [[kami]] of [[Kitabatake Akiyoshi]]. It is one of the [[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]].
==See also== *[[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]
==External links== *[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2928.html Prefectural tourism website]

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Kitabatake Shrine | image = Kitabatake Shrine.jpg | caption = Kitabatake Shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.51858|136.29885|type:landmark_region:JP|format=dms|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = 1643 | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} '''Kitabatake Shrine''' (北畠神社, ''Kitabatake jinja'') is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in [[Tsu, Mie]] Prefecture, Japan. Its main festival is held annually on October 13. It was founded in 1643, and enshrines the [[kami]] of [[Kitabatake Akiyoshi]] as well as other members of the [[Kitabatake clan]]. It is one of the [[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]].
==Tage Kitabatakeshi Jōkan (Kitabatake clan`s residence and castle)== [[File:Kiriyama Castle ruins.jpg|thumb|left|200px|Honmaru compound of Kiriyama Castle]] [[File:北畠氏館跡庭園.jpg|thumb|left|200px|Kitabatake clan's garden]] Kitabatakeshi Yakata was fortified residence of the Tage [[Kitabatake clan]]. The site was designated a National Historic Site.<ref>{{cite web|url= http://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/contents/1001000011242/index.html |title=続日本100名城 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}}</ref> It was the original base of power for the Kitabatake clan who were feudal lords of [[Ise Province]]. But later [[Kitabatake Tomonori]] moved the base to [[Okawachi Castle]].<ref>{{cite web|url= https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8C%97%E7%95%A0%E5%85%B7%E6%95%99-50938 |title=北畠具教 |publisher=コトバンク|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}}</ref> Now Kitabatake Shrine is on site. But the garden made by Kitabatake clan is still remains.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/183/ |title=北畠氏館跡庭園|publisher=津市観光協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}}</ref> Also Kitabatake clan built Kiriyama Castle on the mountain behind Kitabatakeshi Yakata( Kiriyama castle was used in emergencies.).<ref>{{cite web|url=https://kojodan.jp/castle/1255/ |title=北畠氏館跡|publisher=攻城団|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}}</ref>Kiriyama Castle is now only ruins with some earthen walls and dry moat.<ref>{{cite web|url= https://shirobito.jp/castle/1719 |title=霧山城 |publisher=城びと|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}}</ref>
Kitabatakeshi Jōkan (Kitabatake clan's residence and castle) was listed as one of the [[Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles]] in 2017.<ref>{{cite web|url= http://jokaku.jp/japan-top-100-castles/best-100-castles-of-japan-2nd-selection/ |title=続日本100名城 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}}</ref>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)]] *[[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== *[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2928.html Prefectural tourism website]
{{Shinto shrine}} {{Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles}}

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Kitabatake Jinja
北畠神社 | image = Kitabatake Shrine.jpg | caption = Kitabatake Jinja | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.51858|136.29885|type:landmark_region:JP|format=dms|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Kitabatake Akiyoshi]] | founded_by = | established = 1643 | date_destroyed = | location = Tsu-shi, Mie-ken | website = | architecture_style = | festival = October 13 | leadership = | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Palace of Scenic Beauty]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kitabatake Shrine'''|北畠神社| Kitabatake Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in the Misugi neighborhood of the city of [[Tsu, Mie|Tsu]], [[Mie Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is one of the [[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined is the deified spirit of the imperial loyalist [[Kitabatake Akiyoshi]] and other members of the [[Kitabatake clan]]. The shrine's main festival is held annually on October 13. ==History== The shrine is located on the site of the Kitabatakeshi Yakata, a fortified residence of the Tage Kitabatake clan during the [[Nanboku-chō period]]. [[Kitabatake Tomofusa]]'s 4th grandson Suzuki Sonbei Ieji built a small [[Hachiman shrine]] on this site in 1643. Following the [[Meiji restoration]], the new [[Meiji government]] sought out sites connected with the earlier [[Kenmu restoration]] and established a number of Shinto shrines on these locations, both to emphasize the legitimacy of the new regime and to spread the doctrine of [[State Shinto]]. This small Hachiman shrine was rebuilt in November 1881 as the Kitabatake Shrine, and Hachiman was displaced by the deified spirits of Kitabatake Akiyoshi, together with [[Kitabatake Chikafusa]] and [[Kitabatake Akiie]]. In 1907, 16 small shrines from various locations in Tage village were merged into the Kitabatake Shrine, and in 1928 the shrine buildings were reconstructed. The shrine was given then official rank of {{nihongo|''Bekkaku kanpeisha''|別格官幣社||}} under the pre-World War II [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine Is located about ten minutes by car from the [[Hitsu Station]] on the [[JR Central]] [[Meishō Line]]. ==Tage Kitabatakeshi Yakata (Tage Kitabatake clan fortified residence)== [[File:北畠氏館跡庭園.jpg|thumb|rigjt|200px|Kitabatake Jōkan garden]] The Kitabatake clan was a cadet branch of the [[Minamoto clan]] and served closely with the [[Ashikaga shogunate]] and [[Northern Court]] during the wars of the Nanboku-chō period. The clan ruled as [[Kokushi (official)|''kokushi'']] of [[Ise Province]] to the end of the [[Sengoku period]]. They chose the mountainous Tage area which connected [[Yamato Province]] with the Ise Plain to be their stronghold and constructed a fortified residence (''yakata'') on a site that was protected by rivers to the east, south and north, with a steep slope to the west extending to their main redoubt in the mountains, [[Kiriyama Castle]].{{cite web|url=https://kojodan.jp/castle/1255/ |title=北畠氏館跡|publisher=攻城団|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} The residence site is roughly trapezoidal, measuring 200 meters from north-to-south and 110 meters from east-to-west. The northern portion of the site is now occupied by the Kitabatake Shrine.{{cite web|url= http://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/contents/1001000011242/index.html |title=続日本100名城 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} The residence was used until [[Kitabatake Tomonori]] (1528–1576) relocated his seat to [[Okawachi Castle]].{{cite web|url= https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8C%97%E7%95%A0%E5%85%B7%E6%95%99-50938 |title=北畠具教 |publisher=コトバンク|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} However, the [[Japanese garden|gardens]] which were made for the residence have survived.{{cite web|url=http://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/183/ |title=北畠氏館跡庭園|publisher=津市観光協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} In 1936, the ruins of Kiriyama Castle were designated as a National Historic Site, together with the ruins of the Kitabatakeshi Yakata gardens, which were also designated as a National Scenic Site.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162961 |title=多気北畠氏城館跡 / 北畠氏館跡 / 霧山城跡 |trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} The site of the residence was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in 1996, during which time the remains of many buildings from the first half of the 15th century were discovered, along with ceramic shards from China and from many parts of Japan, fragments of armor, and of Buddhist statues and ritual items. In 2006, the National Historic Site designation was expanded to cover the entire Kitabatakeshi Yakata site, including the gardens, Kiriyama Castle and the area connecting the two zones under the name of "Tage Kitabatakeshi Jōkan ato" (Tage Kitabatake clan fortified residences sites). In 2017, the Kitabatakeshi Jōkan was listed as one of the [[Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles]] in 2017.{{cite web|url= http://jokaku.jp/japan-top-100-castles/best-100-castles-of-japan-2nd-selection/ |title=続日本100名城 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} ==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)]] *[[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] *[[List of Shinto shrines]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category-inline|Kitabatake Shrine}} *[https://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/sp/contents/1001000000027/index.html Tsu City tourist information Kitabatakeshi Jōkan Gardens Official website] {{in lang|ja}} * https://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/contents/1001000011242/index.html Tsu City tourist information Kitabatakeshi Jōkan Official website] {{in lang|ja}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Castles in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Tsu, Mie]] [[Category:Ruined castles in Japan]] [[Category:Sengoku period]]

北畠神社 | image = Kitabatake Shrine.jpg | caption = Kitabatake Jinja | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.51858|136.29885|type:landmark_region:JP|format=dms|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Kitabatake Akiyoshi]] | founded_by = | established = 1643 | date_destroyed = | location = Tsu-shi, Mie-ken | website = | architecture_style = | festival = October 13 | leadership = | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Palace of Scenic Beauty]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kitabatake Shrine'''|北畠神社| Kitabatake Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in the Misugi neighborhood of the city of [[Tsu, Mie|Tsu]], [[Mie Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is one of the [[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined is the deified spirit of the imperial loyalist [[Kitabatake Akiyoshi]] and other members of the [[Kitabatake clan]]. [[Kitabatake Tomofusa]]'s 4th grandson Suzuki Sonbei Ieji built a small [[Hachiman shrine]] on this site in 1643. Following the [[Meiji restoration]], the new [[Meiji government]] sought out sites connected with the earlier [[Kenmu restoration]] and established a number of Shinto shrines on these locations, both to emphasize the legitimacy of the new regime and to spread the doctrine of [[State Shinto]]. This small Hachiman shrine was rebuilt in November 1881 as the Kitabatake Shrine, and Hachiman was displaced by the deified spirits of Kitabatake Akiyoshi, together with [[Kitabatake Chikafusa]] and [[Kitabatake Akiie]]. In 1907, 16 small shrines from various locations in Tage village were merged into the Kitabatake Shrine, and in 1928 the shrine buildings were reconstructed. The shrine was given then official rank of {{nihongo|''Bekkaku kanpeisha''|別格官幣社||}} under the pre-World War II [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine Is located about ten minutes by car from the [[Hitsu Station]] on the [[JR Central]] [[Meishō Line]]. ==Tage Kitabatakeshi Yakata (Tage Kitabatake clan fortified residence)== [[File:北畠氏館跡庭園.jpg|thumb|rigjt|200px|Kitabatake Jōkan garden]] The Kitabatake clan was a cadet branch of the [[Minamoto clan]] and served closely with the [[Southern Court]] during the wars of the Nanboku-chō period, later reaching a accord with the [[Ashikaga shogunate]]. The clan ruled as [[Kokushi (official)|''kokushi'']] of [[Ise Province]] to the end of the [[Sengoku period]]. They chose the mountainous Tage area which connected [[Yamato Province]] with the Ise Plain to be their stronghold and constructed a fortified residence (''yakata'') on a site that was protected by rivers to the east, south and north, with a steep slope to the west extending to their main redoubt in the mountains, [[Kiriyama Castle]].{{cite web|url=https://kojodan.jp/castle/1255/ |title=北畠氏館跡|publisher=攻城団|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} The residence site is roughly trapezoidal, measuring 200 meters from north-to-south and 110 meters from east-to-west. The northern portion of the site is now occupied by the Kitabatake Shrine.{{cite web|url= http://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/contents/1001000011242/index.html |title=続日本100名城 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} The residence was used until [[Kitabatake Tomonori]] (1528–1576) relocated his seat to [[Okawachi Castle]].{{cite web|url= https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8C%97%E7%95%A0%E5%85%B7%E6%95%99-50938 |title=北畠具教 |publisher=コトバンク|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} However, the [[Japanese garden|gardens]] which were made for the residence have survived.{{cite web|url=http://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/183/ |title=北畠氏館跡庭園|publisher=津市観光協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} In 1936, the ruins of Kiriyama Castle were designated as a National Historic Site, together with the ruins of the Kitabatakeshi Yakata gardens, which were also designated as a National Scenic Site.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162961 |title=多気北畠氏城館跡 / 北畠氏館跡 / 霧山城跡 |trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} The site of the residence was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in 1996, during which time the remains of many buildings from the first half of the 15th century were discovered, along with ceramic shards from China and from many parts of Japan, fragments of armor, and of Buddhist statues and ritual items. In 2006, the National Historic Site designation was expanded to cover the entire Kitabatakeshi Yakata site, including the gardens, Kiriyama Castle and the area connecting the two zones under the name of "Tage Kitabatakeshi Jōkan ato" (Tage Kitabatake clan fortified residences sites). The total area after the integration and addition was 268,906.91square meters. In 2017, the Kitabatakeshi Jōkan was listed as one of the [[Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles]] in 2017.{{cite web|url= http://jokaku.jp/japan-top-100-castles/best-100-castles-of-japan-2nd-selection/ |title=続日本100名城 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} ==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)]] *[[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] *[[List of Shinto shrines]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category-inline|Kitabatake Shrine}} *[https://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/sp/contents/1001000000027/index.html Tsu City tourist information Kitabatakeshi Jōkan Gardens Official website] {{in lang|ja}} * https://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/contents/1001000011242/index.html Tsu City tourist information Kitabatakeshi Jōkan Official website] {{in lang|ja}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Castles in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Tsu, Mie]] [[Category:Ruined castles in Japan]] [[Category:Sengoku period]]

{{Infobox religious building | name = Kitabatake Jinja
北畠神社 | image = Kitabatake Shrine.jpg | caption = Kitabatake Jinja | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.51858|136.29885|type:landmark_region:JP|format=dms|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Kitabatake Akiyoshi]] | founded_by = | established = 1643 | date_destroyed = | location = Tsu-shi, Mie-ken | website = | architecture_style = | festival = October 13 | leadership = | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Palace of Scenic Beauty]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kitabatake Shrine'''|北畠神社| Kitabatake Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in the Misugi neighborhood of the city of [[Tsu, Mie|Tsu]], [[Mie Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. The clan ruled as [[Kokushi (official)|''kokushi'']] of [[Ise Province]] to the end of the [[Sengoku period]]. They chose the mountainous Tage area which connected [[Yamato Province]] with the Ise Plain to be their stronghold and constructed a fortified residence (''yakata'') on a site that was protected by rivers to the east, south and north, with a steep slope to the west extending to their main redoubt in the mountains, [[Kiriyama Castle]].{{cite web|url=https://kojodan.jp/castle/1255/ |title=北畠氏館跡|publisher=攻城団|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} The residence site is roughly trapezoidal, measuring 200 meters from north-to-south and 110 meters from east-to-west. The northern portion of the site is now occupied by the Kitabatake Shrine.{{cite web|url= http://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/contents/1001000011242/index.html |title=続日本100名城 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} The residence was used until [[Kitabatake Tomonori]] (1528–1576) relocated his seat to [[Okawachi Castle]].{{cite web|url= https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8C%97%E7%95%A0%E5%85%B7%E6%95%99-50938 |title=北畠具教 |publisher=コトバンク|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} However, the [[Japanese garden|gardens]] which were made for the residence have survived.{{cite web|url=http://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/183/ |title=北畠氏館跡庭園|publisher=津市観光協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} In 1936, the ruins of Kiriyama Castle were designated as a National Historic Site, together with the ruins of the Kitabatakeshi Yakata gardens, which were also designated as a National Scenic Site.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162961 |title=多気北畠氏城館跡 / 北畠氏館跡 / 霧山城跡 |trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} The site of the residence was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in 1996, during which time the remains of many buildings from the first half of the 15th century were discovered, along with ceramic shards from China and from many parts of Japan, fragments of armor, and of Buddhist statues and ritual items. In 2006, the National Historic Site designation was expanded to cover the entire Kitabatakeshi Yakata site, including the gardens, Kiriyama Castle and the area connecting the two zones under the name of "Tage Kitabatakeshi Jōkan ato" (Tage Kitabatake clan fortified residences sites). The total area after the integration and addition was 268,906.91square meters. In 2017, the Kitabatakeshi Jōkan was listed as one of the [[Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles]] in 2017.{{cite web|url= http://jokaku.jp/japan-top-100-castles/best-100-castles-of-japan-2nd-selection/ |title=続日本100名城 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} ==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)]] *[[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] *[[List of Shinto shrines]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category-inline|Kitabatake Shrine}} *[https://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/sp/contents/1001000000027/index.html Tsu City tourist information Kitabatakeshi Jōkan Gardens Official website] {{in lang|ja}} * https://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/contents/1001000011242/index.html Tsu City tourist information Kitabatakeshi Jōkan Official website] {{in lang|ja}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Castles in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Tsu, Mie]] [[Category:Ruined castles in Japan]] [[Category:Sengoku period]]

revision.comment: /* History */
revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Kitabatake Jinja
北畠神社 | image = Kitabatake Shrine.jpg | caption = Kitabatake Jinja | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.51858|136.29885|type:landmark_region:JP|format=dms|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Kitabatake Akiyoshi]] | founded_by = | established = 1643 | date_destroyed = | location = Tsu-shi, Mie-ken | website = | architecture_style = | festival = October 13 | leadership = | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Palace of Scenic Beauty]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kitabatake Shrine'''|北畠神社| Kitabatake Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in the Misugi neighborhood of the city of [[Tsu, Mie|Tsu]], [[Mie Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is one of the [[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined is the deified spirit of the imperial loyalist [[Kitabatake Akiyoshi]] and other members of the [[Kitabatake clan]]. The shrine's main festival is held annually on October 13. ==History==[[Category:Beppo Shrines]] The shrine is located on the site of the Kitabatakeshi Yakata, a fortified residence of the Tage Kitabatake clan during the [[Nanboku-chō period]]. [[Kitabatake Tomofusa]]'s 4th grandson Suzuki Sonbei Ieji built a small [[Hachiman shrine]] on this site in 1643. Following the [[Meiji restoration]], the new [[Meiji government]] sought out sites connected with the earlier [[Kenmu restoration]] and established a number of Shinto shrines on these locations, both to emphasize the legitimacy of the new regime and to spread the doctrine of [[State Shinto]]. This small Hachiman shrine was rebuilt in November 1881 as the Kitabatake Shrine, and Hachiman was displaced by the deified spirits of Kitabatake Akiyoshi, together with [[Kitabatake Chikafusa]] and [[Kitabatake Akiie]]. In 1907, 16 small shrines from various locations in Tage village were merged into the Kitabatake Shrine, and in 1928 the shrine buildings were reconstructed. The shrine was given then official rank of {{nihongo|''Bekkaku kanpeisha''|別格官幣社||}} under the pre-World War II [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine Is located about ten minutes by car from the [[Hitsu Station]] on the [[JR Central]] [[Meishō Line]]. ==Tage Kitabatakeshi Yakata (Tage Kitabatake clan fortified residence)== [[File:北畠氏館跡庭園.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Kitabatake Jōkan garden]] The Kitabatake clan was a cadet branch of the [[Minamoto clan]] and served closely with the [[Southern Court]] during the wars of the Nanboku-chō period, later reaching a accord with the [[Ashikaga shogunate]]. The clan ruled as [[Kokushi (official)|''kokushi'']] of [[Ise Province]] to the end of the [[Sengoku period]]. They chose the mountainous Tage area which connected [[Yamato Province]] with the Ise Plain to be their stronghold and constructed a fortified residence (''yakata'') on a site that was protected by rivers to the east, south and north, with a steep slope to the west extending to their main redoubt in the mountains, [[Kiriyama Castle]].{{cite web|url=https://kojodan.jp/castle/1255/ |title=北畠氏館跡|publisher=攻城団|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} The residence site is roughly trapezoidal, measuring 200 meters from north-to-south and 110 meters from east-to-west. The northern portion of the site is now occupied by the Kitabatake Shrine.{{cite web|url= http://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/contents/1001000011242/index.html |title=続日本100名城 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} The residence was used until [[Kitabatake Tomonori]] (1528–1576) relocated his seat to [[Okawachi Castle]].{{cite web|url= https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8C%97%E7%95%A0%E5%85%B7%E6%95%99-50938 |title=北畠具教 |publisher=コトバンク|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} However, the [[Japanese garden|gardens]] which were made for the residence have survived.{{cite web|url=http://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/183/ |title=北畠氏館跡庭園|publisher=津市観光協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} In 1936, the ruins of Kiriyama Castle were designated as a National Historic Site, together with the ruins of the Kitabatakeshi Yakata gardens, which were also designated as a National Scenic Site.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162961 |title=多気北畠氏城館跡 / 北畠氏館跡 / 霧山城跡 |trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} The site of the residence was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in 1996, during which time the remains of many buildings from the first half of the 15th century were discovered, along with ceramic shards from China and from many parts of Japan, fragments of armor, and of Buddhist statues and ritual items. In 2006, the National Historic Site designation was expanded to cover the entire Kitabatakeshi Yakata site, including the gardens, Kiriyama Castle and the area connecting the two zones under the name of "Tage Kitabatakeshi Jōkan ato" (Tage Kitabatake clan fortified residences sites). The total area after the integration and addition was 268,906.91square meters. In 2017, the Kitabatakeshi Jōkan was listed as one of the [[Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles]] in 2017.{{cite web|url= http://jokaku.jp/japan-top-100-castles/best-100-castles-of-japan-2nd-selection/ |title=続日本100名城 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} ==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)]] *[[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] *[[List of Shinto shrines]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category-inline|Kitabatake Shrine}} *[https://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/sp/contents/1001000000027/index.html Tsu City tourist information Kitabatakeshi Jōkan Gardens Official website] {{in lang|ja}} * https://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/contents/1001000011242/index.html Tsu City tourist information Kitabatakeshi Jōkan Official website] {{in lang|ja}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Castles in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Tsu, Mie]] [[Category:Ruined castles in Japan]] [[Category:Sengoku period]]

The shrine was given then official rank of {{nihongo|''Bekkaku kanpeisha''|別格官幣社||}} under the pre-World War II [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine Is located about ten minutes by car from the [[Hitsu Station]] on the [[JR Central]] [[Meishō Line]]. ==Tage Kitabatakeshi Yakata (Tage Kitabatake clan fortified residence)== [[File:北畠氏館跡庭園.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Kitabatake Jōkan garden]] The Kitabatake clan was a cadet branch of the [[Minamoto clan]] and served closely with the [[Southern Court]] during the wars of the Nanboku-chō period, later reaching a accord with the [[Ashikaga shogunate]]. The clan ruled as [[Kokushi (official)|''kokushi'']] of [[Ise Province]] to the end of the [[Sengoku period]]. They chose the mountainous Tage area which connected [[Yamato Province]] with the Ise Plain to be their stronghold and constructed a fortified residence (''yakata'') on a site that was protected by rivers to the east, south and north, with a steep slope to the west extending to their main redoubt in the mountains, [[Kiriyama Castle]].{{cite web|url=https://kojodan.jp/castle/1255/ |title=北畠氏館跡|publisher=攻城団|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} The residence site is roughly trapezoidal, measuring 200 meters from north-to-south and 110 meters from east-to-west. The northern portion of the site is now occupied by the Kitabatake Shrine.{{cite web|url= http://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/contents/1001000011242/index.html |title=続日本100名城 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} The residence was used until [[Kitabatake Tomonori]] (1528–1576) relocated his seat to [[Okawachi Castle]].{{cite web|url= https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8C%97%E7%95%A0%E5%85%B7%E6%95%99-50938 |title=北畠具教 |publisher=コトバンク|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} However, the [[Japanese garden|gardens]] which were made for the residence have survived.{{cite web|url=http://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/183/ |title=北畠氏館跡庭園|publisher=津市観光協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} In 1936, the ruins of Kiriyama Castle were designated as a National Historic Site, together with the ruins of the Kitabatakeshi Yakata gardens, which were also designated as a National Scenic Site.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162961 |title=多気北畠氏城館跡 / 北畠氏館跡 / 霧山城跡 |trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} The site of the residence was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in 1996, during which time the remains of many buildings from the first half of the 15th century were discovered, along with ceramic shards from China and from many parts of Japan, fragments of armor, and of Buddhist statues and ritual items. In 2006, the National Historic Site designation was expanded to cover the entire Kitabatakeshi Yakata site, including the gardens, Kiriyama Castle and the area connecting the two zones under the name of "Tage Kitabatakeshi Jōkan ato" (Tage Kitabatake clan fortified residences sites). The total area after the integration and addition was 268,906.91square meters. In 2017, the Kitabatakeshi Jōkan was listed as one of the [[Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles]] in 2017.{{cite web|url= http://jokaku.jp/japan-top-100-castles/best-100-castles-of-japan-2nd-selection/ |title=続日本100名城 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} ==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)]] *[[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] *[[List of Shinto shrines]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category-inline|Kitabatake Shrine}} *[https://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/sp/contents/1001000000027/index.html Tsu City tourist information Kitabatakeshi Jōkan Gardens Official website] {{in lang|ja}} * https://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/contents/1001000011242/index.html Tsu City tourist information Kitabatakeshi Jōkan Official website] {{in lang|ja}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Castles in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Tsu, Mie]] [[Category:Ruined castles in Japan]] [[Category:Sengoku period]]

It is one of the [[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined is the deified spirit of the imperial loyalist [[Kitabatake Akiyoshi]] and other members of the [[Kitabatake clan]]. The clan ruled as [[Kokushi (official)|''kokushi'']] of [[Ise Province]] to the end of the [[Sengoku period]]. The northern portion of the site is now occupied by the Kitabatake Shrine.{{cite web|url= http://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/contents/1001000011242/index.html |title=続日本100名城 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} The residence was used until [[Kitabatake Tomonori]] (1528–1576) relocated his seat to [[Okawachi Castle]].{{cite web|url= https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8C%97%E7%95%A0%E5%85%B7%E6%95%99-50938 |title=北畠具教 |publisher=コトバンク|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} However, the [[Japanese garden|gardens]] which were made for the residence have survived.{{cite web|url=http://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/183/ |title=北畠氏館跡庭園|publisher=津市観光協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} In 1936, the ruins of Kiriyama Castle were designated as a National Historic Site, together with the ruins of the Kitabatakeshi Yakata gardens, which were also designated as a National Scenic Site.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162961 |title=多気北畠氏城館跡 / 北畠氏館跡 / 霧山城跡 |trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} The site of the residence was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in 1996, during which time the remains of many buildings from the first half of the 15th century were discovered, along with ceramic shards from China and from many parts of Japan, fragments of armor, and of Buddhist statues and ritual items. The total area after the integration and addition was 268,906.91square meters. In 2017, the Kitabatakeshi Jōkan was listed as one of the [[Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles]] in 2017.{{cite web|url= http://jokaku.jp/japan-top-100-castles/best-100-castles-of-japan-2nd-selection/ |title=続日本100名城 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} ==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)]] *[[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] *[[List of Shinto shrines]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category-inline|Kitabatake Shrine}} *[https://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/sp/contents/1001000000027/index.html Tsu City tourist information Kitabatakeshi Jōkan Gardens Official website] {{in lang|ja}} * https://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/contents/1001000011242/index.html Tsu City tourist information Kitabatakeshi Jōkan Official website] {{in lang|ja}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Castles in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Tsu, Mie]] [[Category:Ruined castles in Japan]] [[Category:Sengoku period]] [[Category:Beppo Shrines]]

It is one of the [[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined is the deified spirit of the imperial loyalist [[Kitabatake Akiyoshi]] and other members of the [[Kitabatake clan]]. The shrine's main festival is held annually on October 13. ==History== The shrine is located on the site of the Kitabatakeshi Yakata, a fortified residence of the Tage Kitabatake clan during the [[Nanboku-chō period]]. [[Kitabatake Tomofusa]]'s 4th grandson Suzuki Sonbei Ieji built a small [[Hachiman shrine]] on this site in 1643. Following the [[Meiji restoration]], the new [[Meiji government]] sought out sites connected with the earlier [[Kenmu restoration]] and established a number of Shinto shrines on these locations, both to emphasize the legitimacy of the new regime and to spread the doctrine of [[State Shinto]]. This small Hachiman shrine was rebuilt in November 1881 as the Kitabatake Shrine, and Hachiman was displaced by the deified spirits of Kitabatake Akiyoshi, together with [[Kitabatake Chikafusa]] and [[Kitabatake Akiie]]. In 1907, 16 small shrines from various locations in Tage village were merged into the Kitabatake Shrine, and in 1928 the shrine buildings were reconstructed. The shrine was given then official rank of {{nihongo|''Bekkaku kanpeisha''|別格官幣社||}} under the pre-World War II [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine Is located about ten minutes by car from the [[Hitsu Station]] on the [[JR Central]] [[Meishō Line]]. ==Tage Kitabatakeshi Yakata (Tage Kitabatake clan fortified residence)== [[File:北畠氏館跡庭園.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Kitabatake Jōkan garden]] The Kitabatake clan was a cadet branch of the [[Minamoto clan]] and served closely with the [[Southern Court]] during the wars of the Nanboku-chō period, later reaching an accord with the [[Ashikaga shogunate]]. The clan ruled as [[Kokushi (official)|''kokushi'']] of [[Ise Province]] to the end of the [[Sengoku period]]. They chose the mountainous Tage area which connected [[Yamato Province]] with the Ise Plain to be their stronghold and constructed a fortified residence (''yakata'') on a site that was protected by rivers to the east, south and north, with a steep slope to the west extending to their main redoubt in the mountains, [[Kiriyama Castle]].{{cite web|url=https://kojodan.jp/castle/1255/ |title=北畠氏館跡|publisher=攻城団|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} The residence site is roughly trapezoidal, measuring 200 meters from north-to-south and 110 meters from east-to-west. The northern portion of the site is now occupied by the Kitabatake Shrine.{{cite web|url= http://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/contents/1001000011242/index.html |title=続日本100名城 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} The residence was used until [[Kitabatake Tomonori]] (1528–1576) relocated his seat to [[Okawachi Castle]].{{cite web|url= https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8C%97%E7%95%A0%E5%85%B7%E6%95%99-50938 |title=北畠具教 |publisher=コトバンク|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} However, the [[Japanese garden|gardens]] which were made for the residence have survived.{{cite web|url=http://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/183/ |title=北畠氏館跡庭園|publisher=津市観光協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} In 1936, the ruins of Kiriyama Castle were designated as a National Historic Site, together with the ruins of the Kitabatakeshi Yakata gardens, which were also designated as a National Scenic Site.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162961 |title=多気北畠氏城館跡 / 北畠氏館跡 / 霧山城跡 |trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} The site of the residence was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in 1996, during which time the remains of many buildings from the first half of the 15th century were discovered, along with ceramic shards from China and from many parts of Japan, fragments of armor, and of Buddhist statues and ritual items. In 2006, the National Historic Site designation was expanded to cover the entire Kitabatakeshi Yakata site, including the gardens, Kiriyama Castle and the area connecting the two zones under the name of "Tage Kitabatakeshi Jōkan ato" (Tage Kitabatake clan fortified residences sites). The total area after the integration and addition was 268,906.91square meters. In 2017, the Kitabatakeshi Jōkan was listed as one of the [[Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles]] in 2017.{{cite web|url= http://jokaku.jp/japan-top-100-castles/best-100-castles-of-japan-2nd-selection/ |title=続日本100名城 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} ==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)]] *[[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] *[[List of Shinto shrines]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category-inline|Kitabatake Shrine}} *[https://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/sp/contents/1001000000027/index.html Tsu City tourist information Kitabatakeshi Jōkan Gardens Official website] {{in lang|ja}} * https://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/contents/1001000011242/index.html Tsu City tourist information Kitabatakeshi Jōkan Official website] {{in lang|ja}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Castles in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Tsu, Mie]] [[Category:Ruined castles in Japan]] [[Category:Sengoku period]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]

In 2017, the Kitabatakeshi Jōkan was listed as one of the [[Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles]] in 2017.{{cite web|url= http://jokaku.jp/japan-top-100-castles/best-100-castles-of-japan-2nd-selection/ |title=続日本100名城 |date=29 November 2017 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} ==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)]] *[[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] *[[List of Shinto shrines]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category-inline|Kitabatake Shrine}} *[https://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/sp/contents/1001000000027/index.html Tsu City tourist information Kitabatakeshi Jōkan Gardens Official website] {{in lang|ja}} * https://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/contents/1001000011242/index.html Tsu City tourist information Kitabatakeshi Jōkan Official website] {{in lang|ja}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Castles in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Tsu, Mie]] [[Category:Ruined castles in Japan]] [[Category:Sengoku period]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shrine in Mie Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kitabatake Jinja
北畠神社 | image = Kitabatake Shrine.jpg | caption = Kitabatake Jinja | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.51858|136.29885|type:landmark_region:JP|format=dms|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Kitabatake Akiyoshi]] | founded_by = | established = 1643 | date_destroyed = | location = Tsu-shi, Mie-ken | website = | architecture_style = | festival = October 13 | leadership = | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Palace of Scenic Beauty]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kitabatake Shrine'''|北畠神社| Kitabatake Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in the Misugi neighborhood of the city of [[Tsu, Mie|Tsu]], [[Mie Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is one of the [[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined is the deified spirit of the imperial loyalist [[Kitabatake Akiyoshi]] and other members of the [[Kitabatake clan]]. The shrine's main festival is held annually on October 13. ==History== The shrine is located on the site of the Kitabatakeshi Yakata, a fortified residence of the Tage Kitabatake clan during the [[Nanboku-chō period]]. [[Kitabatake Tomofusa]]'s 4th grandson Suzuki Sonbei Ieji built a small [[Hachiman shrine]] on this site in 1643. Following the [[Meiji restoration]], the new [[Meiji government]] sought out sites connected with the earlier [[Kenmu restoration]] and established a number of Shinto shrines on these locations, both to emphasize the legitimacy of the new regime and to spread the doctrine of [[State Shinto]]. This small Hachiman shrine was rebuilt in November 1881 as the Kitabatake Shrine, and Hachiman was displaced by the deified spirits of Kitabatake Akiyoshi, together with [[Kitabatake Chikafusa]] and [[Kitabatake Akiie]]. In 1907, 16 small shrines from various locations in Tage village were merged into the Kitabatake Shrine, and in 1928 the shrine buildings were reconstructed. The shrine was given then official rank of {{nihongo|''Bekkaku kanpeisha''|別格官幣社||}} under the pre-World War II [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine Is located about ten minutes by car from the [[Hitsu Station]] on the [[JR Central]] [[Meishō Line]]. ==Tage Kitabatakeshi Yakata (Tage Kitabatake clan fortified residence)== [[File:北畠氏館跡庭園.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Kitabatake Jōkan garden]] The Kitabatake clan was a cadet branch of the [[Minamoto clan]] and served closely with the [[Southern Court]] during the wars of the Nanboku-chō period, later reaching an accord with the [[Ashikaga shogunate]]. The clan ruled as [[Kokushi (official)|''kokushi'']] of [[Ise Province]] to the end of the [[Sengoku period]]. They chose the mountainous Tage area which connected [[Yamato Province]] with the Ise Plain to be their stronghold and constructed a fortified residence (''yakata'') on a site that was protected by rivers to the east, south and north, with a steep slope to the west extending to their main redoubt in the mountains, [[Kiriyama Castle]].{{cite web|url=https://kojodan.jp/castle/1255/ |title=北畠氏館跡|publisher=攻城団|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} The residence site is roughly trapezoidal, measuring 200 meters from north-to-south and 110 meters from east-to-west. The northern portion of the site is now occupied by the Kitabatake Shrine.{{cite web|url= http://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/contents/1001000011242/index.html |title=続日本100名城 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} The residence was used until [[Kitabatake Tomonori]] (1528–1576) relocated his seat to [[Okawachi Castle]].{{cite web|url= https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8C%97%E7%95%A0%E5%85%B7%E6%95%99-50938 |title=北畠具教 |publisher=コトバンク|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} However, the [[Japanese garden|gardens]] which were made for the residence have survived.{{cite web|url=http://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/183/ |title=北畠氏館跡庭園|publisher=津市観光協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} In 1936, the ruins of Kiriyama Castle were designated as a National Historic Site, together with the ruins of the Kitabatakeshi Yakata gardens, which were also designated as a National Scenic Site.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162961 |title=多気北畠氏城館跡 / 北畠氏館跡 / 霧山城跡 |trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} The site of the residence was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in 1996, during which time the remains of many buildings from the first half of the 15th century were discovered, along with ceramic shards from China and from many parts of Japan, fragments of armor, and of Buddhist statues and ritual items. In 2006, the National Historic Site designation was expanded to cover the entire Kitabatakeshi Yakata site, including the gardens, Kiriyama Castle and the area connecting the two zones under the name of "Tage Kitabatakeshi Jōkan ato" (Tage Kitabatake clan fortified residences sites). The total area after the integration and addition was 268,906.91square meters. In 2017, the Kitabatakeshi Jōkan was listed as one of the [[Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles]] in 2017.{{cite web|url= http://jokaku.jp/japan-top-100-castles/best-100-castles-of-japan-2nd-selection/ |title=続日本100名城 |date=29 November 2017 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} ==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)]] *[[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] *[[List of Shinto shrines]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category-inline|Kitabatake Shrine}} *[https://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/sp/contents/1001000000027/index.html Tsu City tourist information Kitabatakeshi Jōkan Gardens Official website] {{in lang|ja}} * https://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/contents/1001000011242/index.html Tsu City tourist information Kitabatakeshi Jōkan Official website] {{in lang|ja}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Castles in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Tsu, Mie]] [[Category:Ruined castles in Japan]] [[Category:Sengoku period]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]]

The northern portion of the site is now occupied by the Kitabatake Shrine.{{cite web|url= http://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/contents/1001000011242/index.html |title=続日本100名城 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} The residence was used until [[Kitabatake Tomonori]] (1528–1576) relocated his seat to [[Okawachi Castle]].{{cite web|url= https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8C%97%E7%95%A0%E5%85%B7%E6%95%99-50938 |title=北畠具教 |publisher=コトバンク|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} However, the [[Japanese garden|gardens]] which were made for the residence have survived.{{cite web|url=http://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/183/ |title=北畠氏館跡庭園|publisher=津市観光協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} In 1936, the ruins of Kiriyama Castle were designated as a National Historic Site, together with the ruins of the Kitabatakeshi Yakata gardens, which were also designated as a National Scenic Site.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162961 |title=多気北畠氏城館跡 / 北畠氏館跡 / 霧山城跡 |trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} The site of the residence was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in 1996, during which time the remains of many buildings from the first half of the 15th century were discovered, along with ceramic shards from China and from many parts of Japan, fragments of armor, and of Buddhist statues and ritual items. In 2006, the National Historic Site designation was expanded to cover the entire Kitabatakeshi Yakata site, including the gardens, Kiriyama Castle and the area connecting the two zones under the name of "Tage Kitabatakeshi Jōkan ato" (Tage Kitabatake clan fortified residences sites). The total area after the integration and addition was 268,906.91square meters. In 2017, the Kitabatakeshi Jōkan was listed as one of the [[Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles]] in 2017.{{cite web|url= http://jokaku.jp/japan-top-100-castles/best-100-castles-of-japan-2nd-selection/ |title=続日本100名城 |date=29 November 2017 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} ==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)]] *[[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] *[[List of Shinto shrines]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category-inline|Kitabatake Shrine}} *[https://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/sp/contents/1001000000027/index.html Tsu City tourist information Kitabatakeshi Jōkan Gardens Official website] {{in lang|ja}} * https://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/contents/1001000011242/index.html Tsu City tourist information Kitabatakeshi Jōkan Official website] {{in lang|ja}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Castles in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Tsu, Mie]] [[Category:Ruined castles in Japan]] [[Category:Sengoku period]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[en:Kitabatake Shrine]][[Category:Shinto]][[en:Kitabatake Shrine]]

{{Short description|Shrine in Mie Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kitabatake Jinja
北畠神社 | image = Kitabatake Shrine.jpg | caption = Kitabatake Jinja | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.51858|136.29885|type:landmark_region:JP|format=dms|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Kitabatake Akiyoshi]] | founded_by = | established = 1643 | date_destroyed = | location = Tsu-shi, Mie-ken | website = | architecture_style = | festival = October 13 | leadership = | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Palace of Scenic Beauty]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kitabatake Shrine'''|北畠神社| Kitabatake Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in the Misugi neighborhood of the city of [[Tsu, Mie|Tsu]], [[Mie Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is one of the [[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined is the deified spirit of the imperial loyalist [[Kitabatake Akiyoshi]] and other members of the [[Kitabatake clan]]. The shrine's main festival is held annually on October 13. ==History== The shrine is located on the site of the Kitabatakeshi Yakata, a fortified residence of the Tage Kitabatake clan during the [[Nanboku-chō period]]. [[Kitabatake Tomofusa]]'s 4th grandson Suzuki Sonbei Ieji built a small [[Hachiman shrine]] on this site in 1643. Following the [[Meiji restoration]], the new [[Meiji government]] sought out sites connected with the earlier [[Kenmu restoration]] and established a number of Shinto shrines on these locations, both to emphasize the legitimacy of the new regime and to spread the doctrine of [[State Shinto]]. This small Hachiman shrine was rebuilt in November 1881 as the Kitabatake Shrine, and Hachiman was displaced by the deified spirits of Kitabatake Akiyoshi, together with [[Kitabatake Chikafusa]] and [[Kitabatake Akiie]]. In 1907, 16 small shrines from various locations in Tage village were merged into the Kitabatake Shrine, and in 1928 the shrine buildings were reconstructed. The shrine was given then official rank of {{nihongo|''Bekkaku kanpeisha''|別格官幣社||}} under the pre-World War II [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine Is located about ten minutes by car from the [[Hitsu Station]] on the [[JR Central]] [[Meishō Line]]. ==Tage Kitabatakeshi Yakata (Tage Kitabatake clan fortified residence)== [[File:北畠氏館跡庭園.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Kitabatake Jōkan garden]] The Kitabatake clan was a cadet branch of the [[Minamoto clan]] and served closely with the [[Southern Court]] during the wars of the Nanboku-chō period, later reaching an accord with the [[Ashikaga shogunate]]. The clan ruled as [[Kokushi (official)|''kokushi'']] of [[Ise Province]] to the end of the [[Sengoku period]]. They chose the mountainous Tage area which connected [[Yamato Province]] with the Ise Plain to be their stronghold and constructed a fortified residence (''yakata'') on a site that was protected by rivers to the east, south and north, with a steep slope to the west extending to their main redoubt in the mountains, [[Kiriyama Castle]].{{cite web|url=https://kojodan.jp/castle/1255/ |title=北畠氏館跡|publisher=攻城団|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} The residence site is roughly trapezoidal, measuring 200 meters from north-to-south and 110 meters from east-to-west. The northern portion of the site is now occupied by the Kitabatake Shrine.{{cite web|url= http://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/contents/1001000011242/index.html |title=続日本100名城 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} The residence was used until [[Kitabatake Tomonori]] (1528–1576) relocated his seat to [[Okawachi Castle]].{{cite web|url= https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8C%97%E7%95%A0%E5%85%B7%E6%95%99-50938 |title=北畠具教 |publisher=コトバンク|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} However, the [[Japanese garden|gardens]] which were made for the residence have survived.{{cite web|url=http://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/183/ |title=北畠氏館跡庭園|publisher=津市観光協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} In 1936, the ruins of Kiriyama Castle were designated as a National Historic Site, together with the ruins of the Kitabatakeshi Yakata gardens, which were also designated as a National Scenic Site.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162961 |title=多気北畠氏城館跡 / 北畠氏館跡 / 霧山城跡 |trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} The site of the residence was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in 1996, during which time the remains of many buildings from the first half of the 15th century were discovered, along with ceramic shards from China and from many parts of Japan, fragments of armor, and of Buddhist statues and ritual items. In 2006, the National Historic Site designation was expanded to cover the entire Kitabatakeshi Yakata site, including the gardens, Kiriyama Castle and the area connecting the two zones under the name of "Tage Kitabatakeshi Jōkan ato" (Tage Kitabatake clan fortified residences sites). The total area after the integration and addition was 268,906.91square meters. In 2017, the Kitabatakeshi Jōkan was listed as one of the [[Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles]] in 2017.{{cite web|url= http://jokaku.jp/japan-top-100-castles/best-100-castles-of-japan-2nd-selection/ |title=続日本100名城 |date=29 November 2017 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} ==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)]] *[[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] *[[List of Shinto shrines]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category-inline|Kitabatake Shrine}} *[https://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/sp/contents/1001000000027/index.html Tsu City tourist information Kitabatakeshi Jōkan Gardens Official website] {{in lang|ja}} * https://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/contents/1001000011242/index.html Tsu City tourist information Kitabatakeshi Jōkan Official website] {{in lang|ja}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Castles in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Tsu, Mie]] [[Category:Ruined castles in Japan]] [[Category:Sengoku period]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[en:Kitabatake Shrine]][[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:pages from wikipedia]][[en:Kitabatake Shrine]][[Category:Shinto]] [[en:Kitabatake Shrine]]

{{Short description|Shrine in Mie Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kitabatake Jinja
北畠神社 | image = Kitabatake Shrine.jpg | caption = Kitabatake Jinja | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.51858|136.29885|type:landmark_region:JP|format=dms|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Kitabatake Akiyoshi]] | founded_by = | established = 1643 | date_destroyed = | location = Tsu-shi, Mie-ken | website = | architecture_style = | festival = October 13 | leadership = | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Palace of Scenic Beauty]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kitabatake Shrine'''|北畠神社| Kitabatake Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in the Misugi neighborhood of the city of [[Tsu, Mie|Tsu]], [[Mie Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is one of the [[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined is the deified spirit of the imperial loyalist [[Kitabatake Akiyoshi]] and other members of the [[Kitabatake clan]]. The shrine's main festival is held annually on October 13. ==History== The shrine is located on the site of the Kitabatakeshi Yakata, a fortified residence of the Tage Kitabatake clan during the [[Nanboku-chō period]]. [[Kitabatake Tomofusa]]'s 4th grandson Suzuki Sonbei Ieji built a small [[Hachiman shrine]] on this site in 1643. Following the [[Meiji restoration]], the new [[Meiji government]] sought out sites connected with the earlier [[Kenmu restoration]] and established a number of Shinto shrines on these locations, both to emphasize the legitimacy of the new regime and to spread the doctrine of [[State Shinto]]. This small Hachiman shrine was rebuilt in November 1881 as the Kitabatake Shrine, and Hachiman was displaced by the deified spirits of Kitabatake Akiyoshi, together with [[Kitabatake Chikafusa]] and [[Kitabatake Akiie]]. In 1907, 16 small shrines from various locations in Tage village were merged into the Kitabatake Shrine, and in 1928 the shrine buildings were reconstructed. The shrine was given then official rank of {{nihongo|''Bekkaku kanpeisha''|別格官幣社||}} under the pre-World War II [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine Is located about ten minutes by car from the [[Hitsu Station]] on the [[JR Central]] [[Meishō Line]]. ==Tage Kitabatakeshi Yakata (Tage Kitabatake clan fortified residence)== [[File:北畠氏館跡庭園.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Kitabatake Jōkan garden]] The Kitabatake clan was a cadet branch of the [[Minamoto clan]] and served closely with the [[Southern Court]] during the wars of the Nanboku-chō period, later reaching an accord with the [[Ashikaga shogunate]]. The clan ruled as [[Kokushi (official)|''kokushi'']] of [[Ise Province]] to the end of the [[Sengoku period]]. They chose the mountainous Tage area which connected [[Yamato Province]] with the Ise Plain to be their stronghold and constructed a fortified residence (''yakata'') on a site that was protected by rivers to the east, south and north, with a steep slope to the west extending to their main redoubt in the mountains, [[Kiriyama Castle]].{{cite web|url=https://kojodan.jp/castle/1255/ |title=北畠氏館跡|publisher=攻城団|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} The residence site is roughly trapezoidal, measuring 200 meters from north-to-south and 110 meters from east-to-west. The northern portion of the site is now occupied by the Kitabatake Shrine.{{cite web|url= http://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/contents/1001000011242/index.html |title=続日本100名城 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} The residence was used until [[Kitabatake Tomonori]] (1528–1576) relocated his seat to [[Okawachi Castle]].{{cite web|url= https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8C%97%E7%95%A0%E5%85%B7%E6%95%99-50938 |title=北畠具教 |publisher=コトバンク|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} However, the [[Japanese garden|gardens]] which were made for the residence have survived.{{cite web|url=http://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/183/ |title=北畠氏館跡庭園|publisher=津市観光協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} In 1936, the ruins of Kiriyama Castle were designated as a National Historic Site, together with the ruins of the Kitabatakeshi Yakata gardens, which were also designated as a National Scenic Site.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162961 |title=多気北畠氏城館跡 / 北畠氏館跡 / 霧山城跡 |trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} The site of the residence was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in 1996, during which time the remains of many buildings from the first half of the 15th century were discovered, along with ceramic shards from China and from many parts of Japan, fragments of armor, and of Buddhist statues and ritual items. In 2006, the National Historic Site designation was expanded to cover the entire Kitabatakeshi Yakata site, including the gardens, Kiriyama Castle and the area connecting the two zones under the name of "Tage Kitabatakeshi Jōkan ato" (Tage Kitabatake clan fortified residences sites). The total area after the integration and addition was 268,906.91square meters. In 2017, the Kitabatakeshi Jōkan was listed as one of the [[Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles]] in 2017.{{cite web|url= http://jokaku.jp/japan-top-100-castles/best-100-castles-of-japan-2nd-selection/ |title=続日本100名城 |date=29 November 2017 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} ==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)]] *[[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] *[[List of Shinto shrines]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category-inline|Kitabatake Shrine}} *[https://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/sp/contents/1001000000027/index.html Tsu City tourist information Kitabatakeshi Jōkan Gardens Official website] {{in lang|ja}} * https://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/contents/1001000011242/index.html Tsu City tourist information Kitabatakeshi Jōkan Official website] {{in lang|ja}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Castles in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Tsu, Mie]] [[Category:Ruined castles in Japan]] [[Category:Sengoku period]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[en:Kitabatake Shrine]][[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia1]] [[en:Kitabatake Shrine]][[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia5]][[en:Kitabatake Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia5]] [[en:Kitabatake Shrine]]

The shrine Is located about ten minutes by car from the [[Hitsu Station]] on the [[JR Central]] [[Meishō Line]]. ==Tage Kitabatakeshi Yakata (Tage Kitabatake clan fortified residence)== [[File:北畠氏館跡庭園.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Kitabatake Jōkan garden]] The Kitabatake clan was a cadet branch of the [[Minamoto clan]] and served closely with the [[Southern Court]] during the wars of the Nanboku-chō period, later reaching an accord with the [[Ashikaga shogunate]]. The northern portion of the site is now occupied by the Kitabatake Shrine.{{cite web|url= http://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/contents/1001000011242/index.html |title=続日本100名城 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} The residence was used until [[Kitabatake Tomonori]] (1528–1576) relocated his seat to [[Okawachi Castle]].{{cite web|url= https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8C%97%E7%95%A0%E5%85%B7%E6%95%99-50938 |title=北畠具教 |publisher=コトバンク|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} However, the [[Japanese garden|gardens]] which were made for the residence have survived.{{cite web|url=http://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/183/ |title=北畠氏館跡庭園|publisher=津市観光協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} In 1936, the ruins of Kiriyama Castle were designated as a National Historic Site, together with the ruins of the Kitabatakeshi Yakata gardens, which were also designated as a National Scenic Site.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162961 |title=多気北畠氏城館跡 / 北畠氏館跡 / 霧山城跡 |trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} The site of the residence was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in 1996, during which time the remains of many buildings from the first half of the 15th century were discovered, along with ceramic shards from China and from many parts of Japan, fragments of armor, and of Buddhist statues and ritual items. The northern portion of the site is now occupied by the Kitabatake Shrine.{{cite web|url= http://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/contents/1001000011242/index.html |title=続日本100名城 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} The residence was used until [[Kitabatake Tomonori]] (1528–1576) relocated his seat to [[Okawachi Castle]].{{cite web|url= https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8C%97%E7%95%A0%E5%85%B7%E6%95%99-50938 |title=北畠具教 |publisher=コトバンク|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} However, the [[Japanese garden|gardens]] which were made for the residence have survived.{{cite web|url=http://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/183/ |title=北畠氏館跡庭園|publisher=津市観光協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} In 1936, the ruins of Kiriyama Castle were designated as a National Historic Site, together with the ruins of the Kitabatakeshi Yakata gardens, which were also designated as a National Scenic Site.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162961 |title=多気北畠氏城館跡 / 北畠氏館跡 / 霧山城跡 |trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} The site of the residence was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in 1996, during which time the remains of many buildings from the first half of the 15th century were discovered, along with ceramic shards from China and from many parts of Japan, fragments of armor, and of Buddhist statues and ritual items. In 2006, the National Historic Site designation was expanded to cover the entire Kitabatakeshi Yakata site, including the gardens, Kiriyama Castle and the area connecting the two zones under the name of "Tage Kitabatakeshi Jōkan ato" (Tage Kitabatake clan fortified residences sites). The total area after the integration and addition was 268,906.91square meters. In 2017, the Kitabatakeshi Jōkan was listed as one of the [[Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles]] in 2017.{{cite web|url= http://jokaku.jp/japan-top-100-castles/best-100-castles-of-japan-2nd-selection/ |title=続日本100名城 |date=29 November 2017 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} ==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)]] *[[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] *[[List of Shinto shrines]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category-inline|Kitabatake Shrine}} *[https://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/sp/contents/1001000000027/index.html Tsu City tourist information Kitabatakeshi Jōkan Gardens Official website] {{in lang|ja}} * https://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/contents/1001000011242/index.html Tsu City tourist information Kitabatakeshi Jōkan Official website] {{in lang|ja}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Castles in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Tsu, Mie]] [[Category:Ruined castles in Japan]] [[Category:Sengoku period]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[en:Kitabatake Shrine]][[Category:Shinto]] [[en:Kitabatake Shrine]][[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia5]][[en:Kitabatake Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia5]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia01]] [[en:Kitabatake Shrine]]

{{Short description|Shrine in Mie Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kitabatake Jinja
北畠神社 | image = Kitabatake Shrine.jpg | caption = Kitabatake Jinja | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.51858|136.29885|type:landmark_region:JP|format=dms|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Kitabatake Akiyoshi]] | founded_by = | established = 1643 | date_destroyed = | location = Tsu-shi, Mie-ken | website = | architecture_style = | festival = October 13 | leadership = | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Palace of Scenic Beauty]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kitabatake Shrine'''|北畠神社| Kitabatake Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in the Misugi neighborhood of the city of [[Tsu, Mie|Tsu]], [[Mie Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is one of the [[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined is the deified spirit of the imperial loyalist [[Kitabatake Akiyoshi]] and other members of the [[Kitabatake clan]]. The shrine's main festival is held annually on October 13. ==History== The shrine is located on the site of the Kitabatakeshi Yakata, a fortified residence of the Tage Kitabatake clan during the [[Nanboku-chō period]]. [[Kitabatake Tomofusa]]'s 4th grandson Suzuki Sonbei Ieji built a small [[Hachiman shrine]] on this site in 1643. Following the [[Meiji restoration]], the new [[Meiji government]] sought out sites connected with the earlier [[Kenmu restoration]] and established a number of Shinto shrines on these locations, both to emphasize the legitimacy of the new regime and to spread the doctrine of [[State Shinto]]. This small Hachiman shrine was rebuilt in November 1881 as the Kitabatake Shrine, and Hachiman was displaced by the deified spirits of Kitabatake Akiyoshi, together with [[Kitabatake Chikafusa]] and [[Kitabatake Akiie]]. In 1907, 16 small shrines from various locations in Tage village were merged into the Kitabatake Shrine, and in 1928 the shrine buildings were reconstructed. The shrine was given then official rank of {{nihongo|''Bekkaku kanpeisha''|別格官幣社||}} under the pre-World War II [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine Is located about ten minutes by car from the [[Hitsu Station]] on the [[JR Central]] [[Meishō Line]]. ==Tage Kitabatakeshi Yakata (Tage Kitabatake clan fortified residence)== [[File:北畠氏館跡庭園.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Kitabatake Jōkan garden]] The Kitabatake clan was a cadet branch of the [[Minamoto clan]] and served closely with the [[Southern Court]] during the wars of the Nanboku-chō period, later reaching an accord with the [[Ashikaga shogunate]]. The clan ruled as [[Kokushi (official)|''kokushi'']] of [[Ise Province]] to the end of the [[Sengoku period]]. They chose the mountainous Tage area which connected [[Yamato Province]] with the Ise Plain to be their stronghold and constructed a fortified residence (''yakata'') on a site that was protected by rivers to the east, south and north, with a steep slope to the west extending to their main redoubt in the mountains, [[Kiriyama Castle]].{{cite web|url=https://kojodan.jp/castle/1255/ |title=北畠氏館跡|publisher=攻城団|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} The residence site is roughly trapezoidal, measuring 200 meters from north-to-south and 110 meters from east-to-west. The northern portion of the site is now occupied by the Kitabatake Shrine.{{cite web|url= http://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/contents/1001000011242/index.html |title=続日本100名城 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} The residence was used until [[Kitabatake Tomonori]] (1528–1576) relocated his seat to [[Okawachi Castle]].{{cite web|url= https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8C%97%E7%95%A0%E5%85%B7%E6%95%99-50938 |title=北畠具教 |publisher=コトバンク|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} However, the [[Japanese garden|gardens]] which were made for the residence have survived.{{cite web|url=http://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/183/ |title=北畠氏館跡庭園|publisher=津市観光協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} In 1936, the ruins of Kiriyama Castle were designated as a National Historic Site, together with the ruins of the Kitabatakeshi Yakata gardens, which were also designated as a National Scenic Site.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162961 |title=多気北畠氏城館跡 / 北畠氏館跡 / 霧山城跡 |trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} The site of the residence was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in 1996, during which time the remains of many buildings from the first half of the 15th century were discovered, along with ceramic shards from China and from many parts of Japan, fragments of armor, and of Buddhist statues and ritual items. In 2006, the National Historic Site designation was expanded to cover the entire Kitabatakeshi Yakata site, including the gardens, Kiriyama Castle and the area connecting the two zones under the name of "Tage Kitabatakeshi Jōkan ato" (Tage Kitabatake clan fortified residences sites). The total area after the integration and addition was 268,906.91square meters. In 2017, the Kitabatakeshi Jōkan was listed as one of the [[Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles]] in 2017.{{cite web|url= http://jokaku.jp/japan-top-100-castles/best-100-castles-of-japan-2nd-selection/ |title=続日本100名城 |date=29 November 2017 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} ==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)]] *[[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] *[[List of Shinto shrines]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category-inline|Kitabatake Shrine}} *[https://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/sp/contents/1001000000027/index.html Tsu City tourist information Kitabatakeshi Jōkan Gardens Official website] {{in lang|ja}} * https://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/contents/1001000011242/index.html Tsu City tourist information Kitabatakeshi Jōkan Official website] {{in lang|ja}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Castles in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Tsu, Mie]] [[Category:Ruined castles in Japan]] [[Category:Sengoku period]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[en:Kitabatake Shrine]][[en:Kitabatake Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia5]][[en:Kitabatake Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia5]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia01]] [[en:Kitabatake Shrine]]
