{{Short description|Shrine in Mie Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kitabatake Jinja
北畠神社 | image = Kitabatake Shrine.jpg | caption = Kitabatake Jinja | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.51858|136.29885|type:landmark_region:JP|format=dms|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Kitabatake Akiyoshi]] | founded_by = | established = 1643 | date_destroyed = | location = Tsu-shi, Mie-ken | website = | architecture_style = | festival = October 13 | leadership = | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Palace of Scenic Beauty]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kitabatake Shrine'''|北畠神社| Kitabatake Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in the Misugi neighborhood of the city of [[Tsu, Mie|Tsu]], [[Mie Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is one of the [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined is the deified spirit of the imperial loyalist [[Kitabatake Akiyoshi]] and other members of the [[Kitabatake clan]]. The shrine's main festival is held annually on October 13. ==History== The shrine is located on the site of the Kitabatakeshi Yakata, a fortified residence of the Tage Kitabatake clan during the [[Nanboku-chō period]]. [[Kitabatake Tomofusa]]'s 4th grandson Suzuki Sonbei Ieji built a small [[Hachiman shrine|Hachiman Shrine]] on this site in 1643. Following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], the new [[Meiji government]] sought out sites connected with the earlier [[Kenmu restoration]] and established a number of Shinto shrines on these locations, both to emphasize the legitimacy of the new regime and to spread the doctrine of [[State Shinto]]. This small Hachiman shrine was rebuilt in November 1881 as the Kitabatake Shrine, and Hachiman was displaced by the deified spirits of Kitabatake Akiyoshi, together with [[Kitabatake Chikafusa]] and [[Kitabatake Akiie]]. In 1907, 16 small shrines from various locations in Tage village were merged into the Kitabatake Shrine, and in 1928 the shrine buildings were reconstructed. The shrine was given then official Rank of {{nihongo|''Bekkaku kanpeisha''|別格官幣社||}} under the pre-World War II [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine Is located about ten minutes by car from the [[Hitsu Station]] on the [[JR Central]] [[Meishō Line]]. ==Tage Kitabatakeshi Yakata (Tage Kitabatake clan fortified residence)== [[File:北畠氏館跡庭園.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Kitabatake Jōkan garden]] The Kitabatake clan was a cadet branch of the [[Minamoto clan]] and served closely with the [[Southern Court]] during the wars of the Nanboku-chō period, later reaching an accord with the [[Ashikaga shogunate]]. The clan ruled as [[Kokushi (official)|''kokushi'']] of [[Ise Province]] to the end of the [[Sengoku period]]. They chose the mountainous Tage area which connected [[Yamato Province]] with the Ise Plain to be their stronghold and constructed a fortified residence (''yakata'') on a site that was protected by rivers to the east, south and north, with a steep slope to the west extending to their main redoubt in the mountains, [[Kiriyama Castle]].{{cite web|url=https://kojodan.jp/castle/1255/ |title=北畠氏館跡|publisher=攻城団|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} The residence site is roughly trapezoidal, measuring 200 meters from north-to-south and dd0 meters from east-to-west. The northern portion of the site is now occupied by the Kitabatake Shrine.{{cite web|url= http://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/contents/10010000dd242/index.html |title=続日本100名城 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} The residence was used until [[Kitabatake Tomonori]] (1528–1576) relocated his seat to [[Okawachi Castle]].{{cite web|url= https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8C%97%E7%95%A0%E5%85%B7%E6%95%99-50938 |title=北畠具教 |publisher=コトバンク|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} However, the [[Japanese garden|gardens]] which were made for the residence have survived.{{cite web|url=http://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/183/ |title=北畠氏館跡庭園|publisher=津市観光協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} In 1936, the ruins of Kiriyama Castle were designated as a National Historic Site, together with the ruins of the Kitabatakeshi Yakata gardens, which were also designated as a National Scenic Site.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162961 |title=多気北畠氏城館跡 / 北畠氏館跡 / 霧山城跡 |trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} The site of the residence was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in 1996, during which time the remains of many buildings from the first half of the 15th century were discovered, along with ceramic shards from China and from many parts of Japan, fragments of armor, and of Buddhist statues and ritual items. In 2006, the National Historic Site designation was expanded to cover the entire Kitabatakeshi Yakata site, including the gardens, Kiriyama Castle and the area connecting the two zones under the name of "Tage Kitabatakeshi Jōkan ato" (Tage Kitabatake clan fortified residences sites). The total area after the integration and addition was 268,906.91square meters. In 2017, the Kitabatakeshi Jōkan was listed as one of the [[Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles]] in 2017.{{cite web|url= http://jokaku.jp/japan-top-100-castles/best-100-castles-of-japan-2nd-selection/ |title=続日本100名城 |date=29 November 2017 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} ==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)]] *[[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] *[[List of Shinto shrines]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category-inline|Kitabatake Shrine}} *[https://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/sp/contents/1001000000027/index.html Tsu City tourist information Kitabatakeshi Jōkan Gardens Official website] {{in lang|ja}} * https://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/contents/10010000dd242/index.html Tsu City tourist information Kitabatakeshi Jōkan Official website] {{in lang|ja}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles}} ]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Castles in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Tsu, Mie]] [[Category:Ruined castles in Japan]] [[Category:Sengoku period]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] [[Category:Pages goodbye $1 Wikipedia 7]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[Category:dd 6 4]] [[Category:Expanded pages from Wikipedia]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Pages goodbye $1 Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Kitabatake Shrine]] [[en:Kitabatake Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[arz:ضريح كتاباتاكى]] {{Commons category|Kitabatake Shrine}} [[fr:Kitabatake-jinja]] [[ja:北畠神社]] [[zh:北畠神社]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis]]

The clan ruled as [[Kokushi (official)|''kokushi'']] of [[Ise Province]] to the end of the [[Sengoku period]]. They chose the mountainous Tage area which connected [[Yamato Province]] with the Ise Plain to be their stronghold and constructed a fortified residence (''yakata'') on a site that was protected by rivers to the east, south and north, with a steep slope to the west extending to their main redoubt in the mountains, [[Kiriyama Castle]].{{cite web|url=https://kojodan.jp/castle/1255/ |title=北畠氏館跡|publisher=攻城団|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} The residence site is roughly trapezoidal, measuring 200 meters from north-to-south and dd0 meters from east-to-west. The northern portion of the site is now occupied by the Kitabatake Shrine.{{cite web|url= http://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/contents/10010000dd242/index.html |title=続日本100名城 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} The residence was used until [[Kitabatake Tomonori]] (1528–1576) relocated his seat to [[Okawachi Castle]].{{cite web|url= https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8C%97%E7%95%A0%E5%85%B7%E6%95%99-50938 |title=北畠具教 |publisher=コトバンク|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} However, the [[Japanese garden|gardens]] which were made for the residence have survived.{{cite web|url=http://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/183/ |title=北畠氏館跡庭園|publisher=津市観光協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} In 1936, the ruins of Kiriyama Castle were designated as a National Historic Site, together with the ruins of the Kitabatakeshi Yakata gardens, which were also designated as a National Scenic Site.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162961 |title=多気北畠氏城館跡 / 北畠氏館跡 / 霧山城跡 |trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} The site of the residence was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in 1996, during which time the remains of many buildings from the first half of the 15th century were discovered, along with ceramic shards from China and from many parts of Japan, fragments of armor, and of Buddhist statues and ritual items. In 2006, the National Historic Site designation was expanded to cover the entire Kitabatakeshi Yakata site, including the gardens, Kiriyama Castle and the area connecting the two zones under the name of "Tage Kitabatakeshi Jōkan ato" (Tage Kitabatake clan fortified residences sites). The total area after the integration and addition was 268,906.91square meters. In 2017, the Kitabatakeshi Jōkan was listed as one of the [[Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles]] in 2017.{{cite web|url= http://jokaku.jp/japan-top-100-castles/best-100-castles-of-japan-2nd-selection/ |title=続日本100名城 |date=29 November 2017 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} ==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)]] *[[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] *[[List of Shinto shrines]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category-inline|Kitabatake Shrine}} *[https://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/sp/contents/1001000000027/index.html Tsu City tourist information Kitabatakeshi Jōkan Gardens Official website] {{in lang|ja}} * https://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/contents/10010000dd242/index.html Tsu City tourist information Kitabatakeshi Jōkan Official website] {{in lang|ja}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles}} ]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Castles in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Tsu, Mie]] [[Category:Ruined castles in Japan]] [[Category:Sengoku period]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] [[Category:Pages goodbye $1 Wikipedia 7]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[Category:dd 6 4]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Pages goodbye $1 Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Kitabatake Shrine]] [[en:Kitabatake Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[arz:ضريح كتاباتاكى]] {{Commons category|Kitabatake Shrine}} [[fr:Kitabatake-jinja]] [[ja:北畠神社]] [[zh:北畠神社]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Expanded Pages from Wikipedia]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shrine in Mie Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kitabatake Jinja
北畠神社 | image = Kitabatake Shrine.jpg | caption = Kitabatake Jinja | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.51858|136.29885|type:landmark_region:JP|format=dms|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Kitabatake Akiyoshi]] | founded_by = | established = 1643 | date_destroyed = | location = Tsu-shi, Mie-ken | website = | architecture_style = | festival = October 13 | leadership = | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Palace of Scenic Beauty]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kitabatake Shrine'''|北畠神社| Kitabatake Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in the Misugi neighborhood of the city of [[Tsu, Mie|Tsu]], [[Mie Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is one of the [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined is the deified spirit of the imperial loyalist [[Kitabatake Akiyoshi]] and other members of the [[Kitabatake clan]]. The shrine's main festival is held annually on October 13. ==History== The shrine is located on the site of the Kitabatakeshi Yakata, a fortified residence of the Tage Kitabatake clan during the [[Nanboku-chō period]]. [[Kitabatake Tomofusa]]'s 4th grandson Suzuki Sonbei Ieji built a small [[Hachiman shrine|Hachiman Shrine]] on this site in 1643. Following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], the new [[Meiji government]] sought out sites connected with the earlier [[Kenmu restoration]] and established a number of Shinto shrines on these locations, both to emphasize the legitimacy of the new regime and to spread the doctrine of [[State Shinto]]. This small Hachiman shrine was rebuilt in November 1881 as the Kitabatake Shrine, and Hachiman was displaced by the deified spirits of Kitabatake Akiyoshi, together with [[Kitabatake Chikafusa]] and [[Kitabatake Akiie]]. In 1907, 16 small shrines from various locations in Tage village were merged into the Kitabatake Shrine, and in 1928 the shrine buildings were reconstructed. The shrine was given then official Rank of {{nihongo|''Bekkaku kanpeisha''|別格官幣社||}} under the pre-World War II [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine Is located about ten minutes by car from the [[Hitsu Station]] on the [[JR Central]] [[Meishō Line]]. ==Tage Kitabatakeshi Yakata (Tage Kitabatake clan fortified residence)== [[File:北畠氏館跡庭園.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Kitabatake Jōkan garden]] The Kitabatake clan was a cadet branch of the [[Minamoto clan]] and served closely with the [[Southern Court]] during the wars of the Nanboku-chō period, later reaching an accord with the [[:en:Ashikaga shogunate|Ashikaga shogunate]]. The clan ruled as [[Kokushi (official)|''kokushi'']] of [[Ise Province]] to the end of the [[Sengoku period]]. They chose the mountainous Tage area which connected [[Yamato Province]] with the Ise Plain to be their stronghold and constructed a fortified residence (''yakata'') on a site that was protected by rivers to the east, south and north, with a steep slope to the west extending to their main redoubt in the mountains, [[Kiriyama Castle]].{{cite web|url=https://kojodan.jp/castle/1255/ |title=北畠氏館跡|publisher=攻城団|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} The residence site is roughly trapezoidal, measuring 200 meters from north-to-south and dd0 meters from east-to-west. The northern portion of the site is now occupied by the Kitabatake Shrine.{{cite web|url= http://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/contents/10010000dd242/index.html |title=続日本100名城 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} The residence was used until [[Kitabatake Tomonori]] (1528–1576) relocated his seat to [[Okawachi Castle]].{{cite web|url= https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8C%97%E7%95%A0%E5%85%B7%E6%95%99-50938 |title=北畠具教 |publisher=コトバンク|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} However, the [[Japanese garden|gardens]] which were made for the residence have survived.{{cite web|url=http://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/183/ |title=北畠氏館跡庭園|publisher=津市観光協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} In 1936, the ruins of Kiriyama Castle were designated as a National Historic Site, together with the ruins of the Kitabatakeshi Yakata gardens, which were also designated as a National Scenic Site.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162961 |title=多気北畠氏城館跡 / 北畠氏館跡 / 霧山城跡 |trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} The site of the residence was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in 1996, during which time the remains of many buildings from the first half of the 15th century were discovered, along with ceramic shards from China and from many parts of Japan, fragments of armor, and of Buddhist statues and ritual items. In 2006, the National Historic Site designation was expanded to cover the entire Kitabatakeshi Yakata site, including the gardens, Kiriyama Castle and the area connecting the two zones under the name of "Tage Kitabatakeshi Jōkan ato" (Tage Kitabatake clan fortified residences sites). The total area after the integration and addition was 268,906.91square meters. In 2017, the Kitabatakeshi Jōkan was listed as one of the [[Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles]] in 2017.{{cite web|url= http://jokaku.jp/japan-top-100-castles/best-100-castles-of-japan-2nd-selection/ |title=続日本100名城 |date=29 November 2017 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} ==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)]] *[[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] *[[List of Shinto shrines]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category-inline|Kitabatake Shrine}} *[https://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/sp/contents/1001000000027/index.html Tsu City tourist information Kitabatakeshi Jōkan Gardens Official website] {{in lang|ja}} * https://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/contents/10010000dd242/index.html Tsu City tourist information Kitabatakeshi Jōkan Official website] {{in lang|ja}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles}} ]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Castles in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Tsu, Mie]] [[Category:Ruined castles in Japan]] [[Category:Sengoku period]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[Category:dd 6 4]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[sn:Kitabatake Shrine]] [[en:Kitabatake Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[arz:ضريح كتاباتاكى]] {{Commons category|Kitabatake Shrine}} [[fr:Kitabatake-jinja]] [[ja:北畠神社]] [[zh:北畠神社]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Expanded Pages from Wikipedia]]

In 2017, the Kitabatakeshi Jōkan was listed as one of the [[:en:Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles|Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles]] in 2017.{{cite web|url= http://jokaku.jp/japan-top-100-castles/best-100-castles-of-japan-2nd-selection/ |title=続日本100名城 |date=29 November 2017 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} ==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)]] *[[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] *[[List of Shinto shrines]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category-inline|Kitabatake Shrine}} *[https://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/sp/contents/1001000000027/index.html Tsu City tourist information Kitabatakeshi Jōkan Gardens Official website] {{in lang|ja}} * https://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/contents/10010000dd242/index.html Tsu City tourist information Kitabatakeshi Jōkan Official website] {{in lang|ja}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles}} ]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Castles in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Tsu, Mie]] [[Category:Ruined castles in Japan]] [[Category:Sengoku period]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[Category:dd 6 4]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[sn:Kitabatake Shrine]] [[en:Kitabatake Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[arz:ضريح كتاباتاكى]] {{Commons category|Kitabatake Shrine}} [[fr:Kitabatake-jinja]] [[ja:北畠神社]] [[zh:北畠神社]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis 2]] [[Category:Expanded Pages from Wikipedia]]

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
{{Short description|Shrine in Mie Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kitabatake Jinja
北畠神社 | image = Kitabatake Shrine.jpg | caption = Kitabatake Jinja | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.51858|136.29885|type:landmark_region:JP|format=dms|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Kitabatake Akiyoshi]] | founded_by = | established = 1643 | date_destroyed = | location = Tsu-shi, Mie-ken | website = | architecture_style = | festival = October 13 | leadership = | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Palace of Scenic Beauty]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kitabatake Shrine'''|北畠神社| Kitabatake Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in the Misugi neighborhood of the city of [[Tsu, Mie|Tsu]], [[Mie Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is one of the [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined is the deified spirit of the imperial loyalist [[Kitabatake Akiyoshi]] and other members of the [[Kitabatake clan]]. The shrine's main festival is held annually on October 13. ==History== The shrine is located on the site of the Kitabatakeshi Yakata, a fortified residence of the Tage Kitabatake clan during the [[Nanboku-chō period]]. [[Kitabatake Tomofusa]]'s 4th grandson Suzuki Sonbei Ieji built a small [[Hachiman shrine|Hachiman Shrine]] on this site in 1643. Following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], the new [[Meiji government]] sought out sites connected with the earlier [[Kenmu restoration]] and established a number of Shinto shrines on these locations, both to emphasize the legitimacy of the new regime and to spread the doctrine of [[State Shinto]]. This small Hachiman shrine was rebuilt in November 1881 as the Kitabatake Shrine, and Hachiman was displaced by the deified spirits of Kitabatake Akiyoshi, together with [[Kitabatake Chikafusa]] and [[Kitabatake Akiie]]. In 1907, 16 small shrines from various locations in Tage village were merged into the Kitabatake Shrine, and in 1928 the shrine buildings were reconstructed. The shrine was given then official Rank of {{nihongo|''Bekkaku kanpeisha''|別格官幣社||}} under the pre-World War II [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine Is located about ten minutes by car from the [[:en:Hitsu Station|Hitsu Station]] on the [[JR Central]] [[Meishō Line]]. ==Tage Kitabatakeshi Yakata (Tage Kitabatake clan fortified residence)== [[File:北畠氏館跡庭園.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Kitabatake Jōkan garden]] The Kitabatake clan was a cadet branch of the [[Minamoto clan]] and served closely with the [[Southern Court]] during the wars of the Nanboku-chō period, later reaching an accord with the [[:en:Ashikaga shogunate|Ashikaga shogunate]]. The clan ruled as [[Kokushi (official)|''kokushi'']] of [[Ise Province]] to the end of the [[Sengoku period]]. They chose the mountainous Tage area which connected [[Yamato Province]] with the Ise Plain to be their stronghold and constructed a fortified residence (''yakata'') on a site that was protected by rivers to the east, south and north, with a steep slope to the west extending to their main redoubt in the mountains, [[Kiriyama Castle]].{{cite web|url=https://kojodan.jp/castle/1255/ |title=北畠氏館跡|publisher=攻城団|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} The residence site is roughly trapezoidal, measuring 200 meters from north-to-south and dd0 meters from east-to-west. The northern portion of the site is now occupied by the Kitabatake Shrine.{{cite web|url= http://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/contents/10010000dd242/index.html |title=続日本100名城 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} The residence was used until [[Kitabatake Tomonori]] (1528–1576) relocated his seat to [[Okawachi Castle]].{{cite web|url= https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8C%97%E7%95%A0%E5%85%B7%E6%95%99-50938 |title=北畠具教 |publisher=コトバンク|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} However, the [[Japanese garden|gardens]] which were made for the residence have survived.{{cite web|url=http://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/183/ |title=北畠氏館跡庭園|publisher=津市観光協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} In 1936, the ruins of Kiriyama Castle were designated as a National Historic Site, together with the ruins of the Kitabatakeshi Yakata gardens, which were also designated as a National Scenic Site.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162961 |title=多気北畠氏城館跡 / 北畠氏館跡 / 霧山城跡 |trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} The site of the residence was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in 1996, during which time the remains of many buildings from the first half of the 15th century were discovered, along with ceramic shards from China and from many parts of Japan, fragments of armor, and of Buddhist statues and ritual items. In 2006, the National Historic Site designation was expanded to cover the entire Kitabatakeshi Yakata site, including the gardens, Kiriyama Castle and the area connecting the two zones under the name of "Tage Kitabatakeshi Jōkan ato" (Tage Kitabatake clan fortified residences sites). The total area after the integration and addition was 268,906.91square meters. In 2017, the Kitabatakeshi Jōkan was listed as one of the [[:en:Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles|Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles]] in 2017.{{cite web|url= http://jokaku.jp/japan-top-100-castles/best-100-castles-of-japan-2nd-selection/ |title=続日本100名城 |date=29 November 2017 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} ==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)]] *[[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] *[[List of Shinto shrines]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category-inline|Kitabatake Shrine}} *[https://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/sp/contents/1001000000027/index.html Tsu City tourist information Kitabatakeshi Jōkan Gardens Official website] {{in lang|ja}} * https://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/contents/10010000dd242/index.html Tsu City tourist information Kitabatakeshi Jōkan Official website] {{in lang|ja}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles}} ]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Castles in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Tsu, Mie]] [[Category:Ruined castles in Japan]] [[Category:Sengoku period]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[Category:dd 6 4]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[sn:Kitabatake Shrine]] [[en:Kitabatake Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[arz:ضريح كتاباتاكى]] {{Commons category|Kitabatake Shrine}} [[fr:Kitabatake-jinja]] [[ja:北畠神社]] [[zh:北畠神社]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis 2]] [[Category:Expanded Pages from Wikipedia]]

The northern portion of the site is now occupied by the Kitabatake Shrine.{{cite web|url= http://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/contents/10010000dd242/index.html |title=続日本100名城 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} The residence was used until [[Kitabatake Tomonori]] (1528–1576) relocated his seat to [[Okawachi Castle]].{{cite web|url= https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8C%97%E7%95%A0%E5%85%B7%E6%95%99-50938 |title=北畠具教 |publisher=コトバンク|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} However, the [[Japanese garden|gardens]] which were made for the residence have survived.{{cite web|url=http://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/183/ |title=北畠氏館跡庭園|publisher=津市観光協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} In 1936, the ruins of Kiriyama Castle were designated as a National Historic Site, together with the ruins of the Kitabatakeshi Yakata gardens, which were also designated as a National Scenic Site.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162961 |title=多気北畠氏城館跡 / 北畠氏館跡 / 霧山城跡 |trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} The site of the residence was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in 1996, during which time the remains of many buildings from the first half of the 15th century were discovered, along with ceramic shards from China and from many parts of Japan, fragments of armor, and of Buddhist statues and ritual items. 北畠神社 | image = Kitabatake Shrine.jpg | caption = Kitabatake Jinja | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.51858|136.29885|type:landmark_region:JP|format=dms|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Kitabatake Akiyoshi]] | founded_by = | established = 1643 | date_destroyed = | location = Tsu-shi, Mie-ken | website = | architecture_style = | festival = October 13 | leadership = | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Palace of Scenic Beauty]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kitabatake Shrine'''|北畠神社| Kitabatake Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in the Misugi neighborhood of the city of [[Tsu, Mie|Tsu]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is one of the [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined is the deified spirit of the imperial loyalist [[Kitabatake Akiyoshi]] and other members of the [[Kitabatake clan]]. The shrine's main festival is held annually on October 13. ==History== The shrine is located on the site of the Kitabatakeshi Yakata, a fortified residence of the Tage Kitabatake clan during the [[Nanboku-chō period]]. [[Kitabatake Tomofusa]]'s 4th grandson Suzuki Sonbei Ieji built a small [[Hachiman shrine|Hachiman Shrine]] on this site in 1643. Following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], the new [[Meiji government]] sought out sites connected with the earlier [[Kenmu restoration]] and established a number of Shinto shrines on these locations, both to emphasize the legitimacy of the new regime and to spread the doctrine of [[State Shinto]]. This small Hachiman shrine was rebuilt in November 1881 as the Kitabatake Shrine, and Hachiman was displaced by the deified spirits of Kitabatake Akiyoshi, together with [[Kitabatake Chikafusa]] and [[Kitabatake Akiie]]. In 1907, 16 small shrines from various locations in Tage village were merged into the Kitabatake Shrine, and in 1928 the shrine buildings were reconstructed. The shrine was given then official Rank of {{nihongo|''Bekkaku kanpeisha''|別格官幣社||}} under the pre-World War II [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine Is located about ten minutes by car from the [[:en:Hitsu Station|Hitsu Station]] on the [[JR Central]] [[Meishō Line]]. ==Tage Kitabatakeshi Yakata (Tage Kitabatake clan fortified residence)== [[File:北畠氏館跡庭園.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Kitabatake Jōkan garden]] The Kitabatake clan was a cadet branch of the [[Minamoto clan]] and served closely with the [[Southern Court]] during the wars of the Nanboku-chō period, later reaching an accord with the [[:en:Ashikaga shogunate|Ashikaga shogunate]]. The clan ruled as [[Kokushi (official)|''kokushi'']] of [[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]] to the end of the [[Sengoku period]]. They chose the mountainous Tage area which connected [[Yamato Province]] with the Ise Plain to be their stronghold and constructed a fortified residence (''yakata'') on a site that was protected by rivers to the east, south and north, with a steep slope to the west extending to their main redoubt in the mountains, [[Kiriyama Castle]].{{cite web|url=https://kojodan.jp/castle/1255/ |title=北畠氏館跡|publisher=攻城団|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} The residence site is roughly trapezoidal, measuring 200 meters from north-to-south and dd0 meters from east-to-west. The northern portion of the site is now occupied by the Kitabatake Shrine.{{cite web|url= http://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/contents/10010000dd242/index.html |title=続日本100名城 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} The residence was used until [[Kitabatake Tomonori]] (1528–1576) relocated his seat to [[Okawachi Castle]].{{cite web|url= https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8C%97%E7%95%A0%E5%85%B7%E6%95%99-50938 |title=北畠具教 |publisher=コトバンク|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} However, the [[Japanese garden|gardens]] which were made for the residence have survived.{{cite web|url=http://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/183/ |title=北畠氏館跡庭園|publisher=津市観光協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} In 1936, the ruins of Kiriyama Castle were designated as a National Historic Site, together with the ruins of the Kitabatakeshi Yakata gardens, which were also designated as a National Scenic Site.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162961 |title=多気北畠氏城館跡 / 北畠氏館跡 / 霧山城跡 |trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} The site of the residence was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in 1996, during which time the remains of many buildings from the first half of the 15th century were discovered, along with ceramic shards from China and from many parts of Japan, fragments of armor, and of Buddhist statues and ritual items. In 2006, the National Historic Site designation was expanded to cover the entire Kitabatakeshi Yakata site, including the gardens, Kiriyama Castle and the area connecting the two zones under the name of "Tage Kitabatakeshi Jōkan ato" (Tage Kitabatake clan fortified residences sites). The total area after the integration and addition was 268,906.91square meters. In 2017, the Kitabatakeshi Jōkan was listed as one of the [[:en:Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles|Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles]] in 2017.{{cite web|url= http://jokaku.jp/japan-top-100-castles/best-100-castles-of-japan-2nd-selection/ |title=続日本100名城 |date=29 November 2017 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} ==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)]] *[[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] *[[List of Shinto shrines]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category-inline|Kitabatake Shrine}} *[https://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/sp/contents/1001000000027/index.html Tsu City tourist information Kitabatakeshi Jōkan Gardens Official website] {{in lang|ja}} * https://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/contents/10010000dd242/index.html Tsu City tourist information Kitabatakeshi Jōkan Official website] {{in lang|ja}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles}} ]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Castles in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Tsu, Mie]] [[Category:Ruined castles in Japan]] [[Category:Sengoku period]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[Category:dd 6 4]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[sn:Kitabatake Shrine]] [[en:Kitabatake Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[arz:ضريح كتاباتاكى]] {{Commons category|Kitabatake Shrine}} [[fr:Kitabatake-jinja]] [[ja:北畠神社]] [[zh:北畠神社]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis 2]] [[Category:Expanded Pages from Wikipedia]]

They chose the mountainous Tage area which connected [[Yamato Province]] with the Ise Plain to be their stronghold and constructed a fortified residence (''yakata'') on a site that was protected by rivers to the east, south and north, with a steep slope to the west extending to their main redoubt in the mountains, [[Kiriyama Castle]].{{cite web|url=https://kojodan.jp/castle/1255/ |title=北畠氏館跡|publisher=攻城団|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} The residence site is roughly trapezoidal, measuring 200 meters from north-to-south and dd0 meters from east-to-west. The northern portion of the site is now occupied by the Kitabatake Shrine.{{cite web|url= http://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/contents/10010000dd242/index.html |title=続日本100名城 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} The residence was used until [[Kitabatake Tomonori]] (1528–1576) relocated his seat to [[Okawachi Castle]].{{cite web|url= https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8C%97%E7%95%A0%E5%85%B7%E6%95%99-50938 |title=北畠具教 |publisher=コトバンク|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} However, the [[:en:Japanese garden|gardens]] which were made for the residence have survived.{{cite web|url=http://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/183/ |title=北畠氏館跡庭園|publisher=津市観光協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} In 1936, the ruins of Kiriyama Castle were designated as a National Historic Site, together with the ruins of the Kitabatakeshi Yakata gardens, which were also designated as a National Scenic Site.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162961 |title=多気北畠氏城館跡 / 北畠氏館跡 / 霧山城跡 |trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} The site of the residence was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in 1996, during which time the remains of many buildings from the first half of the 15th century were discovered, along with ceramic shards from China and from many parts of Japan, fragments of armor, and of Buddhist statues and ritual items. In 2006, the National Historic Site designation was expanded to cover the entire Kitabatakeshi Yakata site, including the gardens, Kiriyama Castle and the area connecting the two zones under the name of "Tage Kitabatakeshi Jōkan ato" (Tage Kitabatake clan fortified residences sites). The total area after the integration and addition was 268,906.91square meters. In 2017, the Kitabatakeshi Jōkan was listed as one of the [[:en:Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles|Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles]] in 2017.{{cite web|url= http://jokaku.jp/japan-top-100-castles/best-100-castles-of-japan-2nd-selection/ |title=続日本100名城 |date=29 November 2017 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} ==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)]] *[[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] *[[List of Shinto shrines]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category-inline|Kitabatake Shrine}} *[https://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/sp/contents/1001000000027/index.html Tsu City tourist information Kitabatakeshi Jōkan Gardens Official website] {{in lang|ja}} * https://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/contents/10010000dd242/index.html Tsu City tourist information Kitabatakeshi Jōkan Official website] {{in lang|ja}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles}} ]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Castles in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Tsu, Mie]] [[Category:Ruined castles in Japan]] [[Category:Sengoku period]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[Category:dd 6 4]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[sn:Kitabatake Shrine]] [[en:Kitabatake Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[arz:ضريح كتاباتاكى]] {{Commons category|Kitabatake Shrine}} [[fr:Kitabatake-jinja]] [[ja:北畠神社]] [[zh:北畠神社]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis 2]] [[Category:Expanded Pages from Wikipedia]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shrine in Mie Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kitabatake Jinja
北畠神社 | image = Kitabatake Shrine.jpg | caption = Kitabatake Jinja | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.51858|136.29885|type:landmark_region:JP|format=dms|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Kitabatake Akiyoshi]] | founded_by = | established = 1643 | date_destroyed = | location = Tsu-shi, Mie-ken | website = | architecture_style = | festival = October 13 | leadership = | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Palace of Scenic Beauty]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kitabatake Shrine'''|北畠神社| Kitabatake Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in the Misugi neighborhood of the city of [[Tsu, Mie|Tsu]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is one of the [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined is the deified spirit of the imperial loyalist [[Kitabatake Akiyoshi]] and other members of the [[Kitabatake clan]]. The shrine's main festival is held annually on October 13. ==History== The shrine is located on the site of the Kitabatakeshi Yakata, a fortified residence of the Tage Kitabatake clan during the [[Nanboku-chō period]]. [[Kitabatake Tomofusa]]'s 4th grandson Suzuki Sonbei Ieji built a small [[Hachiman shrine|Hachiman Shrine]] on this site in 1643. Following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], the new [[Meiji government]] sought out sites connected with the earlier [[:en:Kenmu restoration|Kenmu restoration]] and established a number of Shinto shrines on these locations, both to emphasize the legitimacy of the new regime and to spread the doctrine of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]]. This small Hachiman shrine was rebuilt in November 1881 as the Kitabatake Shrine, and Hachiman was displaced by the deified spirits of Kitabatake Akiyoshi, together with [[Kitabatake Chikafusa]] and [[Kitabatake Akiie]]. In 1907, 16 small shrines from various locations in Tage village were merged into the Kitabatake Shrine, and in 1928 the shrine buildings were reconstructed. The shrine was given then official Rank of {{nihongo|''Bekkaku kanpeisha''|別格官幣社||}} under the pre-World War II [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine Is located about ten minutes by car from the [[:en:Hitsu Station|Hitsu Station]] on the [[:en:JR Central|JR Central]] [[Meishō Line]]. ==Tage Kitabatakeshi Yakata (Tage Kitabatake clan fortified residence)== [[File:北畠氏館跡庭園.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Kitabatake Jōkan garden]] The Kitabatake clan was a cadet branch of the [[Minamoto clan]] and served closely with the [[Southern Court]] during the wars of the Nanboku-chō period, later reaching an accord with the [[:en:Ashikaga shogunate|Ashikaga shogunate]]. The clan ruled as [[Kokushi (official)|''kokushi'']] of [[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]] to the end of the [[Sengoku period]]. They chose the mountainous Tage area which connected [[Yamato Province]] with the Ise Plain to be their stronghold and constructed a fortified residence (''yakata'') on a site that was protected by rivers to the east, south and north, with a steep slope to the west extending to their main redoubt in the mountains, [[Kiriyama Castle]].{{cite web|url=https://kojodan.jp/castle/1255/ |title=北畠氏館跡|publisher=攻城団|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} The residence site is roughly trapezoidal, measuring 200 meters from north-to-south and dd0 meters from east-to-west. The northern portion of the site is now occupied by the Kitabatake Shrine.{{cite web|url= http://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/contents/10010000dd242/index.html |title=続日本100名城 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} The residence was used until [[Kitabatake Tomonori]] (1528–1576) relocated his seat to [[Okawachi Castle]].{{cite web|url= https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8C%97%E7%95%A0%E5%85%B7%E6%95%99-50938 |title=北畠具教 |publisher=コトバンク|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} However, the [[:en:Japanese garden|gardens]] which were made for the residence have survived.{{cite web|url=http://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/183/ |title=北畠氏館跡庭園|publisher=津市観光協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} In 1936, the ruins of Kiriyama Castle were designated as a National Historic Site, together with the ruins of the Kitabatakeshi Yakata gardens, which were also designated as a National Scenic Site.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162961 |title=多気北畠氏城館跡 / 北畠氏館跡 / 霧山城跡 |trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} The site of the residence was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in 1996, during which time the remains of many buildings from the first half of the 15th century were discovered, along with ceramic shards from China and from many parts of Japan, fragments of armor, and of Buddhist statues and ritual items. In 2006, the National Historic Site designation was expanded to cover the entire Kitabatakeshi Yakata site, including the gardens, Kiriyama Castle and the area connecting the two zones under the name of "Tage Kitabatakeshi Jōkan ato" (Tage Kitabatake clan fortified residences sites). The total area after the integration and addition was 268,906.91square meters. In 2017, the Kitabatakeshi Jōkan was listed as one of the [[:en:Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles|Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles]] in 2017.{{cite web|url= http://jokaku.jp/japan-top-100-castles/best-100-castles-of-japan-2nd-selection/ |title=続日本100名城 |date=29 November 2017 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} ==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)]] *[[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] *[[List of Shinto shrines]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category-inline|Kitabatake Shrine}} *[https://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/sp/contents/1001000000027/index.html Tsu City tourist information Kitabatakeshi Jōkan Gardens Official website] {{in lang|ja}} * https://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/contents/10010000dd242/index.html Tsu City tourist information Kitabatakeshi Jōkan Official website] {{in lang|ja}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles}} ]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Castles in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Tsu, Mie]] [[Category:Ruined castles in Japan]] [[Category:Sengoku period]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[Category:dd 6 4]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[sn:Kitabatake Shrine]] [[en:Kitabatake Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[arz:ضريح كتاباتاكى]] {{Commons category|Kitabatake Shrine}} [[fr:Kitabatake-jinja]] [[ja:北畠神社]] [[zh:北畠神社]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis 2]] [[Category:Expanded Pages from Wikipedia]]

The total area after the integration and addition was 268,906.91square meters. In 2017, the Kitabatakeshi Jōkan was listed as one of the [[:en:Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles|Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles]] in 2017.{{cite web|url= http://jokaku.jp/japan-top-100-castles/best-100-castles-of-japan-2nd-selection/ |title=続日本100名城 |date=29 November 2017 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} ==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)]] *[[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] *[[List of Shinto shrines]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category-inline|Kitabatake Shrine}} *[https://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/sp/contents/1001000000027/index.html Tsu City tourist information Kitabatakeshi Jōkan Gardens Official website] {{in lang|ja}} * https://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/contents/10010000dd242/index.html Tsu City tourist information Kitabatakeshi Jōkan Official website] {{in lang|ja}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles}} ]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Castles in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Tsu, Mie]] [[Category:Ruined castles in Japan]] [[Category:Sengoku period]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[Category:dd 6 4]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[sn:Kitabatake Shrine]] [[en:Kitabatake Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[arz:ضريح كتاباتاكى]] {{Commons category|Kitabatake Shrine}} [[fr:Kitabatake-jinja]] [[ja:北畠神社]] [[zh:北畠神社]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis 2]] [[Category:Expanded Pages from Wikipedia]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shrine in Mie Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kitabatake Jinja
北畠神社 | image = Kitabatake Shrine.jpg | caption = Kitabatake Jinja | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.51858|136.29885|type:landmark_region:JP|format=dms|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[:en:Kitabatake Akiyoshi|Kitabatake Akiyoshi]] | founded_by = | established = 1643 | date_destroyed = | location = Tsu-shi, Mie-ken | website = | architecture_style = | festival = October 13 | leadership = | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Palace of Scenic Beauty]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kitabatake Shrine'''|北畠神社| Kitabatake Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in the Misugi neighborhood of the city of [[Tsu, Mie|Tsu]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is one of the [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined is the deified spirit of the imperial loyalist [[:en:Kitabatake Akiyoshi|Kitabatake Akiyoshi]] and other members of the [[Kitabatake clan]]. The shrine's main festival is held annually on October 13. ==History== The shrine is located on the site of the Kitabatakeshi Yakata, a fortified residence of the Tage Kitabatake clan during the [[Nanboku-chō period]]. [[Kitabatake Tomofusa]]'s 4th grandson Suzuki Sonbei Ieji built a small [[Hachiman shrine|Hachiman Shrine]] on this site in 1643. Following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], the new [[Meiji government]] sought out sites connected with the earlier [[:en:Kenmu restoration|Kenmu restoration]] and established a number of Shinto shrines on these locations, both to emphasize the legitimacy of the new regime and to spread the doctrine of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]]. This small Hachiman shrine was rebuilt in November 1881 as the Kitabatake Shrine, and Hachiman was displaced by the deified spirits of Kitabatake Akiyoshi, together with [[Kitabatake Chikafusa]] and [[:en:Kitabatake Akiie|Kitabatake Akiie]]. In 1907, 16 small shrines from various locations in Tage village were merged into the Kitabatake Shrine, and in 1928 the shrine buildings were reconstructed. The shrine was given then official Rank of {{nihongo|''Bekkaku kanpeisha''|別格官幣社||}} under the pre-World War II [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine Is located about ten minutes by car from the [[:en:Hitsu Station|Hitsu Station]] on the [[:en:JR Central|JR Central]] [[Meishō Line]]. ==Tage Kitabatakeshi Yakata (Tage Kitabatake clan fortified residence)== [[File:北畠氏館跡庭園.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Kitabatake Jōkan garden]] The Kitabatake clan was a cadet branch of the [[Minamoto clan]] and served closely with the [[Southern Court]] during the wars of the Nanboku-chō period, later reaching an accord with the [[:en:Ashikaga shogunate|Ashikaga shogunate]]. The clan ruled as [[Kokushi (official)|''kokushi'']] of [[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]] to the end of the [[Sengoku period]]. They chose the mountainous Tage area which connected [[Yamato Province]] with the Ise Plain to be their stronghold and constructed a fortified residence (''yakata'') on a site that was protected by rivers to the east, south and north, with a steep slope to the west extending to their main redoubt in the mountains, [[:en:Kiriyama Castle|Kiriyama Castle]].{{cite web|url=https://kojodan.jp/castle/1255/ |title=北畠氏館跡|publisher=攻城団|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} The residence site is roughly trapezoidal, measuring 200 meters from north-to-south and dd0 meters from east-to-west. The northern portion of the site is now occupied by the Kitabatake Shrine.{{cite web|url= http://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/contents/10010000dd242/index.html |title=続日本100名城 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} The residence was used until [[Kitabatake Tomonori]] (1528–1576) relocated his seat to [[Okawachi Castle]].{{cite web|url= https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8C%97%E7%95%A0%E5%85%B7%E6%95%99-50938 |title=北畠具教 |publisher=コトバンク|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} However, the [[:en:Japanese garden|gardens]] which were made for the residence have survived.{{cite web|url=http://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/183/ |title=北畠氏館跡庭園|publisher=津市観光協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} In 1936, the ruins of Kiriyama Castle were designated as a National Historic Site, together with the ruins of the Kitabatakeshi Yakata gardens, which were also designated as a National Scenic Site.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162961 |title=多気北畠氏城館跡 / 北畠氏館跡 / 霧山城跡 |trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} The site of the residence was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in 1996, during which time the remains of many buildings from the first half of the 15th century were discovered, along with ceramic shards from China and from many parts of Japan, fragments of armor, and of Buddhist statues and ritual items. In 2006, the National Historic Site designation was expanded to cover the entire Kitabatakeshi Yakata site, including the gardens, Kiriyama Castle and the area connecting the two zones under the name of "Tage Kitabatakeshi Jōkan ato" (Tage Kitabatake clan fortified residences sites). The total area after the integration and addition was 268,906.91square meters. In 2017, the Kitabatakeshi Jōkan was listed as one of the [[:en:Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles|Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles]] in 2017.{{cite web|url= http://jokaku.jp/japan-top-100-castles/best-100-castles-of-japan-2nd-selection/ |title=続日本100名城 |date=29 November 2017 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} ==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)]] *[[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] *[[List of Shinto shrines]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category-inline|Kitabatake Shrine}} *[https://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/sp/contents/1001000000027/index.html Tsu City tourist information Kitabatakeshi Jōkan Gardens Official website] {{in lang|ja}} * https://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/contents/10010000dd242/index.html Tsu City tourist information Kitabatakeshi Jōkan Official website] {{in lang|ja}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles}} ]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Castles in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Tsu, Mie]] [[Category:Ruined castles in Japan]] [[Category:Sengoku period]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[Category:dd 6 4]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[sn:Kitabatake Shrine]] [[en:Kitabatake Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[arz:ضريح كتاباتاكى]] {{Commons category|Kitabatake Shrine}} [[fr:Kitabatake-jinja]] [[ja:北畠神社]] [[zh:北畠神社]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis 2]] [[Category:Expanded Pages from Wikipedia]]

{{Short description|Shrine in Mie Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kitabatake Jinja
北畠神社 | image = Kitabatake Shrine.jpg | caption = Kitabatake Jinja | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.51858|136.29885|type:landmark_region:JP|format=dms|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[:en:Kitabatake Akiyoshi|Kitabatake Akiyoshi]] | founded_by = | established = 1643 | date_destroyed = | location = Tsu-shi, Mie-ken | website = | architecture_style = | festival = October 13 | leadership = | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Palace of Scenic Beauty]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kitabatake Shrine'''|北畠神社| Kitabatake Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in the Misugi neighborhood of the city of [[Tsu, Mie|Tsu]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is one of the [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined is the deified spirit of the imperial loyalist [[:en:Kitabatake Akiyoshi|Kitabatake Akiyoshi]] and other members of the [[Kitabatake clan]]. The shrine's main festival is held annually on October 13. ==History== The shrine is located on the site of the Kitabatakeshi Yakata, a fortified residence of the Tage Kitabatake clan during the [[Nanboku-chō period]]. [[Kitabatake Tomofusa]]'s 4th grandson Suzuki Sonbei Ieji built a small [[Hachiman shrine|Hachiman Shrine]] on this site in 1643. Following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], the new [[Meiji government]] sought out sites connected with the earlier [[:en:Kenmu restoration|Kenmu restoration]] and established a number of Shinto shrines on these locations, both to emphasize the legitimacy of the new regime and to spread the doctrine of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]]. This small Hachiman shrine was rebuilt in November 1881 as the Kitabatake Shrine, and Hachiman was displaced by the deified spirits of Kitabatake Akiyoshi, together with [[Kitabatake Chikafusa]] and [[:en:Kitabatake Akiie|Kitabatake Akiie]]. In 1907, 16 small shrines from various locations in Tage village were merged into the Kitabatake Shrine, and in 1928 the shrine buildings were reconstructed. The shrine was given then official Rank of {{nihongo|''Bekkaku kanpeisha''|別格官幣社||}} under the pre-World War II [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine Is located about ten minutes by car from the [[:en:Hitsu Station|Hitsu Station]] on the [[:en:JR Central|JR Central]] [[Meishō Line]]. ==Tage Kitabatakeshi Yakata (Tage Kitabatake clan fortified residence)== [[File:北畠氏館跡庭園.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Kitabatake Jōkan garden]] The Kitabatake clan was a cadet branch of the [[Minamoto clan]] and served closely with the [[Southern Court]] during the wars of the Nanboku-chō period, later reaching an accord with the [[:en:Ashikaga shogunate|Ashikaga shogunate]]. The clan ruled as [[:en:Kokushi (official)|''kokushi'']] of [[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]] to the end of the [[Sengoku period]]. They chose the mountainous Tage area which connected [[Yamato Province]] with the Ise Plain to be their stronghold and constructed a fortified residence (''yakata'') on a site that was protected by rivers to the east, south and north, with a steep slope to the west extending to their main redoubt in the mountains, [[:en:Kiriyama Castle|Kiriyama Castle]].{{cite web|url=https://kojodan.jp/castle/1255/ |title=北畠氏館跡|publisher=攻城団|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} The residence site is roughly trapezoidal, measuring 200 meters from north-to-south and dd0 meters from east-to-west. The northern portion of the site is now occupied by the Kitabatake Shrine.{{cite web|url= http://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/contents/10010000dd242/index.html |title=続日本100名城 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} The residence was used until [[:en:Kitabatake Tomonori|Kitabatake Tomonori]] (1528–1576) relocated his seat to [[Okawachi Castle]].{{cite web|url= https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8C%97%E7%95%A0%E5%85%B7%E6%95%99-50938 |title=北畠具教 |publisher=コトバンク|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} However, the [[:en:Japanese garden|gardens]] which were made for the residence have survived.{{cite web|url=http://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/183/ |title=北畠氏館跡庭園|publisher=津市観光協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} In 1936, the ruins of Kiriyama Castle were designated as a National Historic Site, together with the ruins of the Kitabatakeshi Yakata gardens, which were also designated as a National Scenic Site.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162961 |title=多気北畠氏城館跡 / 北畠氏館跡 / 霧山城跡 |trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} The site of the residence was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in 1996, during which time the remains of many buildings from the first half of the 15th century were discovered, along with ceramic shards from China and from many parts of Japan, fragments of armor, and of Buddhist statues and ritual items. In 2006, the National Historic Site designation was expanded to cover the entire Kitabatakeshi Yakata site, including the gardens, Kiriyama Castle and the area connecting the two zones under the name of "Tage Kitabatakeshi Jōkan ato" (Tage Kitabatake clan fortified residences sites). The total area after the integration and addition was 268,906.91square meters. In 2017, the Kitabatakeshi Jōkan was listed as one of the [[:en:Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles|Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles]] in 2017.{{cite web|url= http://jokaku.jp/japan-top-100-castles/best-100-castles-of-japan-2nd-selection/ |title=続日本100名城 |date=29 November 2017 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} ==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)]] *[[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] *[[List of Shinto shrines]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category-inline|Kitabatake Shrine}} *[https://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/sp/contents/1001000000027/index.html Tsu City tourist information Kitabatakeshi Jōkan Gardens Official website] {{in lang|ja}} * https://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/contents/10010000dd242/index.html Tsu City tourist information Kitabatakeshi Jōkan Official website] {{in lang|ja}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles}} ]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Castles in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Tsu, Mie]] [[Category:Ruined castles in Japan]] [[Category:Sengoku period]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[Category:dd 6 4]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[sn:Kitabatake Shrine]] [[en:Kitabatake Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[arz:ضريح كتاباتاكى]] {{Commons category|Kitabatake Shrine}} [[fr:Kitabatake-jinja]] [[ja:北畠神社]] [[zh:北畠神社]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis 2]]

The shrine Is located about ten minutes by car from the [[:en:Hitsu Station|Hitsu Station]] on the [[:en:JR Central|JR Central]] [[Meishō Line]]. ==Tage Kitabatakeshi Yakata (Tage Kitabatake clan fortified residence)== [[File:北畠氏館跡庭園.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Kitabatake Jōkan garden]] The Kitabatake clan was a cadet branch of the [[Minamoto clan]] and served closely with the [[Southern Court]] during the wars of the Nanboku-chō period, later reaching an accord with the [[:en:Ashikaga shogunate|Ashikaga shogunate]]. The clan ruled as [[:en:Kokushi (official)|''kokushi'']] of [[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]] to the end of the [[Sengoku period]]. They chose the mountainous Tage area which connected [[Yamato Province]] with the Ise Plain to be their stronghold and constructed a fortified residence (''yakata'') on a site that was protected by rivers to the east, south and north, with a steep slope to the west extending to their main redoubt in the mountains, [[:en:Kiriyama Castle|Kiriyama Castle]].{{cite web|url=https://kojodan.jp/castle/1255/ |title=北畠氏館跡|publisher=攻城団|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} The residence site is roughly trapezoidal, measuring 200 meters from north-to-south and dd0 meters from east-to-west. The northern portion of the site is now occupied by the Kitabatake Shrine.{{cite web|url= http://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/contents/10010000dd242/index.html |title=続日本100名城 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} The residence was used until [[:en:Kitabatake Tomonori|Kitabatake Tomonori]] (1528–1576) relocated his seat to [[Okawachi Castle]].{{cite web|url= https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8C%97%E7%95%A0%E5%85%B7%E6%95%99-50938 |title=北畠具教 |publisher=コトバンク|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} However, the [[:en:Japanese garden|gardens]] which were made for the residence have survived.{{cite web|url=http://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/183/ |title=北畠氏館跡庭園|publisher=津市観光協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} In 1936, the ruins of Kiriyama Castle were designated as a National Historic Site, together with the ruins of the Kitabatakeshi Yakata gardens, which were also designated as a National Scenic Site.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162961 |title=多気北畠氏城館跡 / 北畠氏館跡 / 霧山城跡 |trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} The site of the residence was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in 1996, during which time the remains of many buildings from the first half of the 15th century were discovered, along with ceramic shards from China and from many parts of Japan, fragments of armor, and of Buddhist statues and ritual items. In 2006, the National Historic Site designation was expanded to cover the entire Kitabatakeshi Yakata site, including the gardens, Kiriyama Castle and the area connecting the two zones under the name of "Tage Kitabatakeshi Jōkan ato" (Tage Kitabatake clan fortified residences sites). The total area after the integration and addition was 268,906.91square meters. In 2017, the Kitabatakeshi Jōkan was listed as one of the [[:en:Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles|Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles]] in 2017.{{cite web|url= http://jokaku.jp/japan-top-100-castles/best-100-castles-of-japan-2nd-selection/ |title=続日本100名城 |date=29 November 2017 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} ==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)]] *[[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] *[[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category-inline|Kitabatake Shrine}} *[https://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/sp/contents/1001000000027/index.html Tsu City tourist information Kitabatakeshi Jōkan Gardens Official website] {{in lang|ja}} * https://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/contents/10010000dd242/index.html Tsu City tourist information Kitabatakeshi Jōkan Official website] {{in lang|ja}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles}} ]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Castles in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Tsu, Mie]] [[Category:Ruined castles in Japan]] [[Category:Sengoku period]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[Category:dd 6 4]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[sn:Kitabatake Shrine]] [[en:Kitabatake Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[arz:ضريح كتاباتاكى]] {{Commons category|Kitabatake Shrine}} [[fr:Kitabatake-jinja]] [[ja:北畠神社]] [[zh:北畠神社]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis 2]]

{{Short description|Shrine in Mie Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kitabatake Jinja
北畠神社 | image = Kitabatake Shrine.jpg | caption = Kitabatake Jinja | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.51858|136.29885|type:landmark_region:JP|format=dms|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[:en:Kitabatake Akiyoshi|Kitabatake Akiyoshi]] | founded_by = | established = 1643 | date_destroyed = | location = Tsu-shi, Mie-ken | website = | architecture_style = | festival = October 13 | leadership = | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Palace of Scenic Beauty]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kitabatake Shrine'''|北畠神社| Kitabatake Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in the Misugi neighborhood of the city of [[Tsu, Mie|Tsu]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is one of the [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined is the deified spirit of the imperial loyalist [[:en:Kitabatake Akiyoshi|Kitabatake Akiyoshi]] and other members of the [[Kitabatake clan]]. The shrine's main festival is held annually on October 13. ==History== The shrine is located on the site of the Kitabatakeshi Yakata, a fortified residence of the Tage Kitabatake clan during the [[Nanboku-chō period]]. [[Kitabatake Tomofusa]]'s 4th grandson Suzuki Sonbei Ieji built a small [[Hachiman shrine|Hachiman Shrine]] on this site in 1643. Following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], the new [[:en:Government of Meiji Japan|Government of Meiji Japan]] sought out sites connected with the earlier [[:en:Kenmu restoration|Kenmu restoration]] and established a number of Shinto shrines on these locations, both to emphasize the legitimacy of the new regime and to spread the doctrine of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]]. This small Hachiman shrine was rebuilt in November 1881 as the Kitabatake Shrine, and Hachiman was displaced by the deified spirits of Kitabatake Akiyoshi, together with [[Kitabatake Chikafusa]] and [[:en:Kitabatake Akiie|Kitabatake Akiie]]. In 1907, 16 small shrines from various locations in Tage village were merged into the Kitabatake Shrine, and in 1928 the shrine buildings were reconstructed. The shrine was given then official Rank of {{nihongo|''Bekkaku kanpeisha''|別格官幣社||}} under the pre-World War II [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine Is located about ten minutes by car from the [[:en:Hitsu Station|Hitsu Station]] on the [[:en:JR Central|JR Central]] [[Meishō Line]]. ==Tage Kitabatakeshi Yakata (Tage Kitabatake clan fortified residence)== [[File:北畠氏館跡庭園.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Kitabatake Jōkan garden]] The Kitabatake clan was a cadet branch of the [[Minamoto clan]] and served closely with the [[Southern Court]] during the wars of the Nanboku-chō period, later reaching an accord with the [[:en:Ashikaga shogunate|Ashikaga shogunate]]. The clan ruled as [[:en:Kokushi (official)|''kokushi'']] of [[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]] to the end of the [[Sengoku period]]. They chose the mountainous Tage area which connected [[Yamato Province]] with the Ise Plain to be their stronghold and constructed a fortified residence (''yakata'') on a site that was protected by rivers to the east, south and north, with a steep slope to the west extending to their main redoubt in the mountains, [[:en:Kiriyama Castle|Kiriyama Castle]].{{cite web|url=https://kojodan.jp/castle/1255/ |title=北畠氏館跡|publisher=攻城団|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} The residence site is roughly trapezoidal, measuring 200 meters from north-to-south and dd0 meters from east-to-west. The northern portion of the site is now occupied by the Kitabatake Shrine.{{cite web|url= http://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/contents/10010000dd242/index.html |title=続日本100名城 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} The residence was used until [[:en:Kitabatake Tomonori|Kitabatake Tomonori]] (1528–1576) relocated his seat to [[Okawachi Castle]].{{cite web|url= https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8C%97%E7%95%A0%E5%85%B7%E6%95%99-50938 |title=北畠具教 |publisher=コトバンク|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} However, the [[:en:Japanese garden|gardens]] which were made for the residence have survived.{{cite web|url=http://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/183/ |title=北畠氏館跡庭園|publisher=津市観光協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} In 1936, the ruins of Kiriyama Castle were designated as a National Historic Site, together with the ruins of the Kitabatakeshi Yakata gardens, which were also designated as a National Scenic Site.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162961 |title=多気北畠氏城館跡 / 北畠氏館跡 / 霧山城跡 |trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} The site of the residence was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in 1996, during which time the remains of many buildings from the first half of the 15th century were discovered, along with ceramic shards from China and from many parts of Japan, fragments of armor, and of Buddhist statues and ritual items. In 2006, the National Historic Site designation was expanded to cover the entire Kitabatakeshi Yakata site, including the gardens, Kiriyama Castle and the area connecting the two zones under the name of "Tage Kitabatakeshi Jōkan ato" (Tage Kitabatake clan fortified residences sites). The total area after the integration and addition was 268,906.91square meters. In 2017, the Kitabatakeshi Jōkan was listed as one of the [[:en:Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles|Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles]] in 2017.{{cite web|url= http://jokaku.jp/japan-top-100-castles/best-100-castles-of-japan-2nd-selection/ |title=続日本100名城 |date=29 November 2017 |publisher=日本城郭協会|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} ==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)]] *[[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] *[[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category-inline|Kitabatake Shrine}} *[https://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/sp/contents/1001000000027/index.html Tsu City tourist information Kitabatakeshi Jōkan Gardens Official website] {{in lang|ja}} * https://www.info.city.tsu.mie.jp/www/contents/10010000dd242/index.html Tsu City tourist information Kitabatakeshi Jōkan Official website] {{in lang|ja}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Continued Top 100 Japanese Castles}} ]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Castles in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Tsu, Mie]] [[Category:Ruined castles in Japan]] [[Category:Sengoku period]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[Category:dd 6 4]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[sn:Kitabatake Shrine]] [[en:Kitabatake Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[arz:ضريح كتاباتاكى]] {{Commons category|Kitabatake Shrine}} [[fr:Kitabatake-jinja]] [[ja:北畠神社]] [[zh:北畠神社]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis 2]]
